questions.question: Why do learning and feelings come from many brain cells instead of one?

options.A: Each brain cell acts alone to form the whole.
options.B: Many brain cells work together to make new patterns.
options.C: Only chemical signals at synapses store information.
options.D: Learning does not change after birth.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to explain teamwork of brain cells.
concepts_tested: Emergence: How complex properties of brains and behavior arise from interactions among neurons, glia, and neural circuits.
Multiscale/integrative approach: Connecting molecular, cellular, system, and cognitive levels using diverse disciplines and techniques.
Brain–behavior relationships: The links between neural mechanisms and learning, memory, perception, behavior, and consciousness.

questions.question: When something harms the body, how do different parts work together?

options.A: Only fast electrical signals reach every cell at once.
options.B: Many signals from nerves, blood, nearby cells, and self signals make parts act together.
options.C: When only one tissue gets signals, each part acts alone.
options.D: Every tissue must have the same receptor for all cells to respond identically.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words, used simple examples, kept short sentences.
concepts_tested: Homeostasis: the regulatory mechanisms that maintain a stable internal environment.
Structure–Function relationship: how anatomical organization at molecular, cellular, and organ levels dictates and supports physiological function.
Multilevel integration and communication: coordination of biophysical/biochemical processes through cell-to-cell signaling to achieve coherent organismal function.

questions.question: How do new behaviors appear when parts work together?

options.A: Each part has traits that only appear when parts join.
options.B: Parts work together to make new patterns no single part can make.
options.C: Boundaries prevent information flow between parts.
options.D: New behaviors are just the average of each part.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to explain how parts make new patterns.
concepts_tested: Emergence and synergy: systems exhibit properties that arise from interactions of parts and are not reducible to individual components.
Boundaries, context, structure, function, and relations: how a system is defined and how its environment and internal organization shape behavior and interactions with other systems.
Adaptation and equifinality: systems learn and adapt depending on engagement with their environment, and different paths can lead to the same end state.

questions.question: Why do new group behaviors appear when many parts follow nearby rules?

options.A: The whole equals the sum of the parts.
options.B: Parts interact in changing ways to create new patterns.
options.C: Averaging all parts makes a single simple trend.
options.D: More parts always make the whole behave in a straight way.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to explain how parts make new group patterns.
concepts_tested: Emergence from interacting components: how non-linear, collective dynamics lead to higher-order behaviors not reducible to individual parts.
Organized vs. disorganized complexity: different methodological approaches (probability/statistical mechanics vs. holistic, multi-factor analysis) applied to different types of complex phenomena.
Relational regimes and state spaces as sources of complexity: complexity arises from the number and variety of distinguishable relationships and their possible states within a system.

questions.question: Why can people use mental pictures and simple steps to solve new problems?

options.A: Mental pictures help make new solutions from learned steps.
options.B: People only react to things.
options.C: Mental pictures are too small to support thinking.
options.D: Thinking only copies senses into actions.

questions.simplification_notes: I changed technical words to 'mental pictures' and 'simple steps'.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The mind is best understood through representational structures and computational procedures that operate on them.
Concept 2: Cognitive phenomena emerge from and can be analyzed across multiple levels, from neural circuitry to modular brain organization and behavior.
Concept 3: Cognitive science is inherently interdisciplinary, integrating psychology, philosophy, AI, neuroscience, linguistics, and anthropology to model and explain cognition.

questions.question: Why does random assignment make cause claims stronger than letting people choose groups?

options.A: Random assignment makes groups exactly the same.
options.B: Random assignment makes groups similar in seen and hidden ways.
options.C: Random assignment removes the need for any statistics.
options.D: Random assignment makes the sample match the whole population.

questions.simplification_notes: I removed hard words and used simple examples about groups and choices.
concepts_tested: Descriptive vs. inferential statistics and how sampling variation affects generalization from a sample to a population
Hypothesis testing framework, including null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, and Type I/Type II errors
Experimental vs. observational study designs and the role of manipulation/randomization in establishing causal inferences and the importance of representative sampling

questions.question: Why do science networks and teams shape how science gets done?

options.A: They give everyone equal resources.
options.B: They let one best person decide all results.
options.C: They help scientists share skills, special tools, hidden know-how, trust and rewards.
options.D: They always make fast breakthroughs without extra work.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified words for third graders. Kept the idea that networks share skills and tools.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Resource allocation and funding as mechanisms to align science with public interest and economic development.
Concept 2: Knowledge networks and collaborations as essential structures shaping how science is produced (distribution of expertise, equipment, and know-how).
Concept 3: The multi-actor, international ecosystem (governments, firms, NGOs, universities) and its influence on science policy and global research agendas.

questions.question: Why do groups use written rules and unwritten habits to guide behavior?

options.A: Written rules always decide what is best for everyone.
options.B: Unwritten habits replace written rules completely.
options.C: Both written rules and unwritten habits guide people by giving clear steps and trust.
options.D: Groups choose rules or habits only by group size.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences.
concepts_tested: The governance framework as a system of rules, laws, norms, and power dynamics that guide decision-making and acceptable conduct.
The role and structure of governing bodies (formal and informal) in enforcing rules and overseeing group operations.
Governance as the allocation and mobilization of resources and the setting of a collective direction to address needs and challenges.

questions.question: How do big rules and people make groups that run a country? What makes those groups stay the same or sometimes change?

options.A: Only rules decide how things turn out.
options.B: Only people decide how things turn out.
options.C: Many small choices make rules that stay the same until big moments change them.
options.D: Only big outside shocks change rules.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words for rules, people, groups, and change.
concepts_tested: Normative vs. positive political science: differences in aims, methods, and what counts as explanation vs. evaluation.
Structure and agency in political analysis: how social structures and individual/aggregate actors interact to shape political institutions and behavior.
Disciplinary boundaries and interdisciplinarity: how political science emerged from and relates to philosophy, history, economics, sociology, and law, and what this means for methods and questions.

questions.question: If all students get computers, why might poor students still score lower?

options.A: Technology alone decides learning success.
options.B: Learning needs family help, good teachers, enough food, useful lessons.
options.C: Students will not use computers without teacher help.
options.D: Giving computers fixes all school problems by itself.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words for third graders. I kept the core idea that many things affect learning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Education as a mechanism of socialization and skill development mediated by formal, non-formal, and informal structures, and its effects on individuals and society.
Concept 2: The aims of education and the tension between fostering critical thinking versus indoctrination, and how these aims influence policy, assessment, and practice.

Concept 3: Determinants and disparities in educational success, including psychological factors (motivation, intelligence, personality), social factors (socioeconomic status, ethnicity, gender), and structural factors (access to technology, teacher quality, parental involvement) and how they interact to produce outcomes.

questions.question: How do rules and social structures decide who gets schools, houses, and rights? Why do these choices keep some people poorer and others richer over time?

options.A: Rules and institutions give power and resources to some groups for many years.
options.B: Rules only show existing inequality without changing access.
options.C: They matter only in small tribal groups rather than in modern countries.
options.D: Only effort decides who gets schools, houses, and rights.

questions.simplification_notes: I replaced big words with simple words and split the question into two short sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Norms of allocation and structural factors determine who gains access to social goods and rights.
Concept 2: Cultural narratives and social identities (e.g., deserving/undeserving) influence beliefs about inequality and help justify or perpetuate it.
Concept 3: Variations in societal organization (egalitarian, ranked, stratified; kinship-oriented vs. materially oriented) affect the level and progression of inequality.

questions.question: What does meritocracy use to choose who gets ahead? What common problem still makes chances unfair?

options.A: It gives jobs by chance.
options.B: It rewards skill and test results; unequal access to schooling limits fairness.
options.C: It promises equal school funding for everyone.
options.D: It makes family ties illegal for equal results.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words; kept idea about skill versus unequal resources.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Merit as measured by demonstrated ability and performance (examinations, IQ, credentials) and its role as the mechanism for advancement
Concept 2: Relationship to equality of opportunity and anti-nepotism/hereditary privilege (meritocracy as a pathway to fair advancement vs. nepotism/aristocracy)
Concept 3: Historical and conceptual complexities (origins of the term, satirical critique, evolving broader usage, and philosophical connections such as Ethos)

questions.question: Patients arrive at random in an emergency room. Why use a computer model instead of a fixed math plan?

options.A: Computer models copy random arrivals and parts that affect each other in the system.
options.B: Computer models find the best staff plan without any data.
options.C: Fixed math plans need less computer work than computer models.
options.D: Computer models remove uncertainty by averaging random changes.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and a hospital example to show randomness and interactions.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mathematical modeling as the foundation of OR—constructing models to describe the system in order to analyze and improve decisions.
Concept 2: Optimization objective—OR aims to maximize or minimize real-world objectives (profit, cost, risk, etc.) to achieve best outcomes.
Concept 3: Method selection workflow—choosing and applying techniques (simulation, optimization, stochastic models, etc.) based on system nature, goals, and computational constraints.

questions.question: Why does one failed test not make scientists drop a theory?

options.A: It assumes data are neutral.
options.B: It says one failed test always proves a theory false.
options.C: Scientists change background ideas instead of the main theory after failed tests.
options.D: It claims progress comes only from simpler theories.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified words. I kept the point about background assumptions and theory change.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Demarcation and justification of scientific knowledge (distinguishing science from non-science; reliability/validity of theories; role of induction)
Concept 2: The nature of scientific progress and theory change (paradigms, scientific revolutions, and how progress can be relative to historical frameworks)
Concept 3: Competing justification frameworks and critique of a universal "scientific method" (logical positivism, falsification, coherence, and Feyerabend’s methodological pluralism)

questions.question: Why must scientists make ideas that tests can show are wrong?

options.A: It makes the idea give the same result every time.
options.B: It makes the idea give predictions that tests could show wrong.
options.C: It makes data prove the idea true over time.
options.D: It forces scientists to match ideas to old beliefs.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words to explain being shown wrong by a test.
concepts_tested: Falsifiability and hypothesis-based testing: hypotheses must be testable with predictions; results can support, refine, or discard the hypothesis.
Iterative, principle-based nature of scientific inquiry: the method is not a fixed step-by-step process; different fields vary, and steps may be omitted or reordered.
Role of empirical observation and skepticism: careful observation and skepticism help prevent cognitive biases from distorting interpretation of data.

questions.question: What does a density over chance $p$ let us do to find expected loss?

options.A: A density over $p$ lets us average losses when $p$ is unknown.
options.B: It treats $p$ as fixed.
options.C: It says we should use only observed frequencies.
options.D: It says all $p$ values are equally likely as a neutral guess.

questions.simplification_notes: Changed technical terms to simple words and used 'average' for integration.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The distinction and relationship between uncertainty, risk, and variability (and how they are used differently in forecasting and decision-making).
Concept 2: How uncertainty is measured using probability distributions and density functions, including the idea of first-order vs. second-order uncertainty (confidence over outcome probabilities).
Concept 3: The role of risk as outcomes with potential losses and how it can be conceptually separate from pure uncertainty (balance between expected values, loss functions, and variability).

questions.question: How does negative feedback keep a system steady?

options.A: It ignores feedback.
options.B: It reduces error by checking output against a goal, adjusting the input.
options.C: It amplifies output changes, making the system run away.
options.D: It removes the link between output and input.

questions.simplification_notes: I used short simple words to show checking output and changing the input.
concepts_tested: Circular causality and feedback loops as a mechanism by which a system’s outputs influence future inputs to maintain or adjust behavior.
Recursion and self-organization as processes that generate complex, adaptive behavior from simpler rules and information processing.
The role of information, control, and communication as fundamental cross-domain functions linking systems in biology, machines, and society.

questions.question: Why does the Earth's surface break into moving plates instead of one whole shell?

options.A: The mantle moves evenly under the shell.
options.B: A strong outer shell on a weak layer breaks into separate plates.
options.C: Surface erosion makes weak zones in the shell.
options.D: Mantle flow makes only vertical motion at the surface.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and kept the main idea that a hard shell sits on a weak layer.
concepts_tested: Mantle convection as the driver of plate tectonics and related geologic phenomena
Rock deformation modes (elastic, plastic, brittle) and how stress magnitude and relaxation times dictate deformation behavior
Internal stress–gravity–density relationships and hydrostatic equilibrium, including the role of the equation of state to model density with depth

questions.question: How do order clues and number ages work together to make rock timelines?

options.A: Both relative and absolute dating give exact ages for rocks.
options.B: Absolute dating gives the actual ages of rocks.
options.C: Relative and absolute dating give both order and numbers for rocks.
options.D: Both methods give the same information about rocks.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard words with simple words and kept the key idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Geology as an integrative, evidence-based reconstruction of Earth's history using multiple methods (fieldwork, rock description, geophysics, chemistry, paleontology, etc.).
Concept 2: Relative vs. absolute dating as complementary tools for building geological timelines (how each method contributes to dating rocks and events).
Concept 3: Plate tectonics as a central unifying framework in geology, supported by rock records, fossils, and climate history, explaining the distribution and evolution of Earth’s systems.

questions.question: How does checking a policy's results help make better rules next time?

options.A: It makes leaders repeat the same rule to keep votes.
options.B: It shows if results met goals, pointing to needed changes.
options.C: It blames others for results to avoid fixing the rule.
options.D: It freezes the original problem idea, blocking new aims.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording for children, keeping idea of using evaluation to guide changes.
concepts_tested: The policy cycle as a framework (agenda setting, formulation, legitimation, implementation, evaluation) and the role of feedback in shaping future policy.
The network of actors and co-production (politicians, civil servants, interest groups, experts, citizens) and how power dynamics and collaboration influence policy design and outcomes.

The link between policy aims and instruments (how regulations, subsidies, taxes, and spending plans are selected and mapped to objectives like economic growth, inequality reduction, or environmental protection).

questions.question: Why can a tiny change in one part make the whole system change big?

options.A: Parts react in proportion to input.
options.B: Loops and tipping points can make a small change become a big change.
options.C: New group behaviors are just the sum of each part.
options.D: The whole system only follows outside rules.

questions.simplification_notes: Used short simple words. Kept idea that feedback and tipping points can amplify small changes.
concepts_tested: Emergence and nonlinear interactions leading to system-wide properties
Feedback loops, self-organization, and adaptation as mechanisms driving dynamics
Network modeling and the holistic, interdisciplinary paradigm that challenges reductionism

questions.question: Why must a science idea make clear predictions that tests could show are wrong?

options.A: Tests show if the idea is wrong.
options.B: It guarantees that tests will prove the idea true with enough data.
options.C: It makes researchers trust their gut instead of data.
options.D: It forces results to agree with old theories.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified vocabulary. I kept the idea of making testable predictions that can be shown wrong.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The scientific method as a systematic framework involving testable hypotheses, predictions, and empirical validation that builds knowledge about the universe.

Concept 2: Relationships among disciplines and their methodologies (natural vs. social sciences; formal sciences; applied sciences) and how these relate to empirical versus deductive approaches and practical use of knowledge.
Concept 3: The historical and social evolution of science (development from natural philosophy to modern science, the role of collaboration, institutions, and policy in shaping scientific practice).

questions.question: How does pay tied to results change worker choice when the boss cannot watch?

options.A: They make pay fixed.
options.B: They tie pay to results the worker can change.
options.C: They remove all monitoring.
options.D: They shift all risk to the worker.

questions.simplification_notes: Made words simple.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Information asymmetry and hidden actions/information (moral hazard and adverse selection) as drivers of the principal–agent problem.

Concept 2: Agency costs and incentive alignment mechanisms (e.g., piece rates, profit sharing, efficiency wages, performance measurement, bonds, termination) used to align agent behavior with principal interests.
Concept 3: The multiple-principal problem and governance/collective-action dynamics when more than one principal must coordinate with an agent (including implications for the public sector).

questions.question: How do clear job rules for managers, the board, and owners help a company?

options.A: It gives managers all power for faster decisions.
options.B: Clear role rules create checks and balances for setting goals and checking managers.
options.C: They make everyone always know every business detail.
options.D: They stop rules for running the company after goals are set.

questions.simplification_notes: Used short simple words. Kept the checks and balances idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Corporate governance as a system of processes, structures, and mechanisms that influence the control and direction of a corporation.

Concept 2: The relationships among key actors (management, board, shareholders, and stakeholders) and how these relationships provide the framework for directing, setting objectives, and monitoring performance.
Concept 3: Governance frameworks based on general principles (e.g., Cadbury, OECD, Sarbanes–Oxley) that guide proper governance and accountability.

questions.question: Why can mixing choices make the other player not want to change their move?

options.A: Mixing makes every move the best choice for each player.
options.B: Mixing makes the opponent get the same average outcome from each move.
options.C: Mixing always gives a single best move for each player.
options.D: Mixing makes players always choose the lowest payoff outcome.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words like move, mixing, and average. I kept the idea that mixing makes different moves give the same result.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Equilibrium concepts in strategic interactions (e.g., mixed-strategy equilibria, Nash equilibrium) and their role in predicting rational behavior.
Concept 2: Classification of games by payoff structure (zero-sum vs non-zero-sum; cooperative vs non-cooperative) and how that classification guides modeling and outcomes.

Concept 3: Mathematical foundations and extensions (fixed-point theorems, axiomatic expected utility, evolutionary game theory) that provide the rigorous basis and broaden applicability of game-theoretic reasoning.

questions.question: Why does a whole system show new behaviors not found in any single part?

options.A: The whole is just the sum of each part.
options.B: Parts interact to make new patterns in the whole.
options.C: New behaviors vanish at smaller scales.
options.D: Outside forces alone cause the new behaviors.

questions.simplification_notes: I replaced hard words with simple words. I kept the main idea about interactions making new patterns.
concepts_tested: Emergence and synergy: systems are "more than the sum of their parts" due to interactions that produce new properties.
Interdependence, boundaries, and context: systems have causal boundaries and are shaped by context, with structure, function, and relations determining behavior.
Adaptation, learning, and equifinality: system growth and adaptation depend on engagement with the environment, allowing multiple paths to similar outcomes.

questions.question: Why does making one machine faster not always help the whole system?

options.A: A faster machine can overload other parts or workers.
options.B: Speed increases always cut energy use.
options.C: Later steps will always adapt perfectly to changes.
options.D: The slowest part alone sets the whole flow.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified words to show that faster parts can cause bottlenecks.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Integrated systems thinking—IE designs and optimizes systems that involve people, materials, information, equipment, and energy, focusing on interactions to specify, predict, and evaluate outcomes.

Concept 2: Process-improvement mechanisms—use of lean manufacturing, Six Sigma, and related methods to reduce waste and enhance quality and productivity, illustrating cause-effect links between practices and performance.
Concept 3: Interdisciplinary synthesis—IE combines engineering, mathematics, and social sciences (along with business principles) to model, analyze, and manage complex processes and organizations.

questions.question: When output goes back to input, how does feedback change stability?

options.A: Positive feedback reinforces changes; negative feedback opposes changes, reduces errors, promotes stability.
options.B: Positive feedback always damps changes; negative feedback always makes changes grow.
options.C: The sign of feedback has no effect on stability; only outside disturbances matter.
options.D: Any delay makes both types of feedback cause instability.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences for third graders.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Feedback loop mechanism — outputs fed back to inputs to form a circuit; reasoning must analyze the system as a whole.
Concept 2: Feedback can regulate or amplify system behavior, with potential for instability (e.g., regeneration in amplifiers, governors controlling speed).
Concept 3: Cross-domain applicability — feedback as evaluative/corrective information that can influence the controlling source (e.g., business feedback).

questions.question: Why does a rule without randomness give only one future path from one start? How does adding randomness change the possible future paths?

options.A: A rule with no randomness gives one next state; randomness makes many possible next states.
options.B: Determinism needs time to be continuous; randomness makes time discrete.
options.C: Determinism needs the state space to be finite; randomness needs infinite states.
options.D: Determinism guarantees convergence to a fixed point; randomness guarantees endless oscillation.

questions.simplification_notes: I shortened the question. I used 'randomness' instead of hard words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Evolution rule and determinism vs stochasticity (how the current state leads to future states, and when randomness influences outcomes)
Concept 2: State space and trajectories/orbits (the state space as the set of possible states and the path that results from evolving over time)
Concept 3: Dynamical systems as a unifying framework (how they relate to ODEs, difference equations, ergodic theory, chaos, and other models)

questions.question: Why does fixing only one part of a big system not help the whole system?

options.A: Because parts work alone.
options.B: Because parts affect each other through feedback, whole system behavior changes.
options.C: Because the whole result equals the sum of parts.
options.D: Because algorithms always split resources evenly.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words. Showed that part interactions and feedback change the whole.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Systems thinking / holistic integration — how components interact to produce emergent system behavior and why a whole-system view is necessary.

Concept 2: Discovery-driven problem identification — why systems engineering begins with identifying real problems and high-impact failures rather than assuming manufacturing-like repetitive optimization.

Concept 3: Life-cycle orientation and cross-disciplinary integration — how design, integration, risk management, testing, maintenance, and decommissioning across multiple disciplines are coordinated to ensure a cohesive system.

questions.question: How do social rules and people's choices make society stay the same or change?

options.A: Social rules always decide what people do.
options.B: People act alone without rules shaping their choices.
options.C: Rules, people and technology form a cycle, keep it stable and let it change.
options.D: Social order happens by chance without rules or people shaping it.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question simple and added technology to show its role.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Structure vs. agency — the interplay between social structure and individual agency in producing social order and social change.
Concept 2: Micro-level and macro-level analysis — how individual interactions aggregate into large-scale social systems and patterns.
Concept 3: Digital sociology and technology’s impact — how digital technologies (e.g., the internet) reshape social relations, networks, and power dynamics.

questions.question: How can breaking rules help a group keep its rules?

options.A: It weakens group beliefs.
options.B: It makes people see rules as always up for change.
options.C: It shows the line between right and wrong, prompting people to support values.
options.D: It pushes rule breakers away to form new groups.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording to child language. Kept idea that breaking rules shows boundaries causing people to reaffirm values.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Informal vs formal social control as mechanisms by which behavior is regulated to maintain social norms and order.
Concept 2: The paradox of deviance in social control (deviance as both regulated by and defining social norms, reinforcing conformity).
Concept 3: The role of institutions (government, religion, civil society) in formal social control to prevent chaos/anomie and sustain social order.

questions.question: How can rules shape society to stay the same or to change?

options.A: When rules guide people, actions repeat; when many people change actions, new rules form.
options.B: Rules only come from people; they never shape what people do later.
options.C: People make rules; existing rules do not affect what people do.
options.D: Change comes only from plans by top leaders; everyday actions do not matter.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words; showed that rules and people shape stability and change.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Structure as both emergent from and determinant of individual action (reciprocal causation)
Concept 2: Multi-level nature of social structure (macro, meso, micro) and cross-level influences (structures, networks, norms, institutions)
Concept 3: Norms and their internalization as a mechanism for sustaining or changing social structure

questions.question: How do pushes on cells change which genes cells use to shape the body?

options.A: Pushes on cells break DNA to make new gene programs.
options.B: Pushes on cells change gene activity via cell glue and skeleton.
options.C: Only chemicals tell cells how to shape bodies.
options.D: Cells pick type first then pushes give no feedback.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard words with simple ones. Kept idea that pushes change genes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mechanical forces and cell movement are integral to morphogenesis and are coupled to genetic regulation.
Concept 2: Morphogen gradients guide patterning and differentiation through signaling and receptor interactions.
Concept 3: Gene regulatory networks and master regulatory genes orchestrate cascades of transcriptional events that control cellular behaviors such as migration, adhesion, and contractility.

questions.question: Why does using three kinds of data give stronger proof of how animals relate?

options.A: It makes all studies give the same tree every time.
options.B: It treats each dataset equally by averaging their signals.
options.C: Different evidence has different errors; agreement raises confidence; disagreement shows where to study.
options.D: It lets scientists drop data that disagree with their chosen idea.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified language; removed technical terms; kept agreement builds confidence; disagreement shows issues.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Phylogenetic trees encode both relationships (branching order) and amount of evolution (branch length); these trees are used to study evolution of traits and biogeography.

Concept 2: Systematics uses multiple data-driven branches (numerical systematics/biometry, biochemical systematics, experimental systematics) that classify organisms based on statistics, molecular/biochemical characteristics, and evolutionary units—complementing each other in revealing evolutionary relationships.

Concept 3: The field has practical applications in documenting biodiversity (extinct and living), taxonomy practices (names, classifications), conservation, and management through biological control, illustrating how understanding phylogeny informs real-world decisions.

questions.question: How do clear goals change your planning, compared to unclear goals?

options.A: Clear-goal problems let you plan steps using small goals; unclear goals need flexible plans.
options.B: Clear-goal problems require frequent goal changes; unclear problems allow fixed plans.
options.C: Shortcuts are not needed for clear problems; unclear problems must avoid shortcuts.
options.D: Planning is the same for both types; only the final check differs.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified wording to compare clear versus unclear goals for planning.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Well-defined vs ill-defined problems and how they shape planning and solution strategies.
Concept 2: Cognitive obstacles to problem solving (e.g., confirmation bias, mental set, functional fixedness) and approaches to overcome them.
Concept 3: The role of resources, knowledge, and domain-specific techniques in enabling problem solving (professional skills, cross-domain methods, and the study of mental processes).

questions.question: Why do people say that pleasure in beauty should be free from personal wants? How does this change how people judge things for beauty?

options.A: It makes beauty judgments depend on usefulness.
options.B: Disinterest makes people judge an object's form rather than personal wants.
options.C: It makes judgments reflect moral rules.
options.D: It requires matching judgments to the artist's intention.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote using simple words about beauty.
concepts_tested: The objectivity vs. subjectivity of aesthetic properties and judgments
Disinterested pleasure as a hallmark of aesthetic experience
The three main approaches to aesthetics (concepts/judgments, experiences/responses, and nature/features of aesthetic objects) and their implications

questions.question: How do ethics change what a company thinks when profit and doing good conflict?

options.A: They replace laws with company rules that let profits have no limits.
options.B: They change expected gains by adding reputation costs that make care count.
options.C: They promise long-term profits by forcing one moral outcome.
options.D: They remove governance with ethics alone making choices.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words to show ethics add reputation costs that change choices.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The normative vs. descriptive dimensions of business ethics and how they guide or describe business behavior.
Concept 2: The relationship between profit-maximizing behavior and non-economic (ethical, social) concerns, and how ethical norms mediate or influence corporate decision-making.
Concept 3: Organizational mechanisms for ethical governance (ethics codes, social responsibility charters, ethics regimes) and their role alongside or beyond legal regulation.

questions.question: How do leaders share power between national and local governments? Why do courts decide fights to keep the country working?

options.A: The national government gets unlimited power over local laws.
options.B: The constitution sits above all governments with courts enforcing uniform laws.
options.C: Independent courts keep fair rule after powers split into exclusive, shared, and leftover parts.
options.D: Local units keep full sovereignty, causing coordination gaps.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Allocation and hierarchy of governmental powers (separation of powers and federal vs. unitary structures)
Concept 2: Sources of constitutional rules (codified vs. uncodified constitutions; role of conventions, statutes, customary law, and international law)
Concept 3: Rights and limits on government action (how constitutions grant powers like taxation/spending while also protecting individual rights)

questions.question: How does the rule of law stop leaders from using power unfairly?

options.A: It lets leaders change rules to fit their aims without limits.
options.B: Written rules and fair courts stop leaders from using power unfairly.
options.C: It favors quick law changes instead of steady predictable rules.
options.D: It says law is only what leaders want, blocking fair checks.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple child words. Replaced 'constitutionalism' with 'written rules'. Replaced 'Rechtsstaat' with 'fair courts'.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Equality before the law and nonarbitrary governance as the core principle of the rule of law
Concept 2: Formalist vs. substantivist conceptions, including how laws should be stable, accessible, clear, and how rights and international law broaden the concept
Concept 3: Relationship to other political-legal ideas (rule of law vs rule of man; links to constitutionalism and Rechtsstaat)

questions.question: Why do natural law supporters say laws must follow moral rules found by reason? Why should laws not just come from custom or rulers' orders?

options.A: Because reason finds moral rules in human nature that make some laws right.
options.B: Because natural law says rulers alone decide what counts as law.
options.C: Because it says right and wrong come only from custom.
options.D: Because it treats law as written rules whose enforcement makes them valid.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and short sentences to show reason gives laws legitimacy instead of rulers or custom.
concepts_tested: Natural law consists of inherent, universal moral standards that can be discovered through reason, grounding intrinsic human rights.

Natural law provides a relationship to positive law by undergirding and informing human-made laws, and it has been used to justify political theories (e.g., social contract) and rights, in opposition to legal positivism.

The historical development and transmission of natural law across thinkers and eras (from ancient philosophers to Aquinas to the Enlightenment) shape its conceptual role in modern rights theory and jurisprudence.

questions.question: Why should we study people's connections instead of only their traits?

options.A: A person's place in a group shapes what they can do.
options.B: People's traits alone decide what happens to them.
options.C: Ties give fixed roles that stop personal choice.
options.D: Spread happens by chance without any role for connections.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I focused on connections shaping outcomes.
concepts_tested: The relational axiom: social phenomena should be understood through the properties of relations (ties) between actors, not solely by the attributes of the actors themselves.

Structure-driven patterns and dynamics: local and global patterns, influence, and the spread of information arise from the network's architecture, linking cause (structure) to effect (patterns/outcomes).
Foundational models and methods: triads, sociograms, and graph-theoretic formalization provide the theoretical and methodological framework for analyzing social networks.

questions.question: How does trust help people with shared rules make things for everyone?

options.A: Formal punishments stop people from breaking rules.
options.B: Shared rules make people expect help and cooperate.
options.C: One trusted leader makes all decisions for the group.
options.D: People follow private interests when no shared rules guide them.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed technical words and history. Focused on trust, shared rules, expected help, and cooperation.
concepts_tested: Social capital as productive networks built on interpersonal relationships, shared identity, norms, and trust that enable cooperative action and generate public goods.
Mechanisms by which social capital affects outcomes (e.g., performance in diverse groups, entrepreneurial firms, managerial performance, supply chain relations) through cooperation and reciprocity.

The broader, historical/philosophical grounding of social capital (associational life, democracy, debates about modernization) as a conceptual framework linking social structure to collective outcomes.

questions.question: How do network models that use chance help us predict real networks?

options.A: They copy real networks exactly from past edges.
options.B: They use simple chance rules for links to predict network patterns.
options.C: They say node traits decide every link.
options.D: They need exact history of all edges to predict new links.

questions.simplification_notes: I removed hard words. I kept the main idea about chance models predicting networks.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Networks as relational structures composed of nodes and edges, enabling representation and analysis of complex phenomena across disciplines.

Concept 2: Graph-theoretic and probabilistic frameworks (e.g., Erdős–Rényi random graphs, network probability models, p*) as mechanisms to model edge formation and network properties to support predictive modeling.

Concept 3: Multidisciplinary evolution of network science (from graph theory to sociograms to predictive network models) showing how theories from mathematics, physics, computer science, statistics, and sociology coalesce to study complex networks.

questions.question: Why can one small change around a child cause big changes in many parts?

options.A: The brain keeps each area separate.
options.B: Many parts affect each other across body, mind, and feelings.
options.C: Each area grows alone.
options.D: Context matters only when you are very young.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and a short question to show how one change can spread.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Nature–nurture interactions and context-dependent development across the lifespan
Concept 2: Interrelation and co-development of physical, cognitive, and social-emotional domains
Concept 3: Theoretical frameworks for development (stage-based vs dynamic-systems) and the role of context (lifespan/ecological factors) in explaining development

questions.question: Why does balance come from each side's ability to resist the other?

options.A: Being seen as right gives power to the stronger person.
options.B: The total power in a system stays fixed.
options.C: Each side's power depends on the other's ability to resist.
options.D: Institutions make power stay the same.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I showed each side can resist the other.
concepts_tested: Power as relational and diffuse (including structural and discursive forms; legitimacy of authority)
Classification of power tactics (soft vs hard, rational vs nonrational, unilateral vs bilateral) and how contexts/personality influence use
Balance of power as a relational constraint framework (relative strengths, stability, and constraint rather than absolute power)

questions.question: Why do groups often find better answers than any one person?

options.A: Members share unique facts to make a stronger combined answer.
options.B: The group follows the loudest person instead of other ideas.
options.C: The group only averages members' answers with no new features.
options.D: Social loafing makes members work less, lowering performance.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I kept the main idea.
concepts_tested: Emergent properties of groups (holism): group behavior and qualities that cannot be deduced from individuals alone.
Group-level mechanisms/forces: positive and negative forces within groups that shape behavior (e.g., status, reciprocity, ostracism, leadership, group decision).
Intergroup relations and social regulation mechanisms: how groups manage cooperation, accountability, and conflict (identifying cheaters, altruism, intergroup dynamics).

questions.question: Why do property rights sometimes have limits? How do laws make sure limits stay fair?

options.A: Because ownership has no social role, governments may take property whenever they want.
options.B: They stop unfair taking while laws allow rules, taxes, or taking for public good.
options.C: Because states must always seize property when public need appears with no fair process.
options.D: Only companies have property rights while people have none.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified words for children while keeping the main idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The right to property comprises the entitlement to own property (individually or in association) and protection against arbitrary deprivation.
Concept 2: Property rights are not absolute; they may be limited by public interest through mechanisms like regulations, taxation, and nationalisation.

Concept 3: The scope and status of property rights are contested and vary by context, including which entities qualify as holders (individuals vs. corporations) and which kinds of property are protected, across different international instruments and regions.

questions.question: Why do skilled workers who learn fast help an economy grow?

options.A: They let factories make many identical things with fewer raw materials.
options.B: They let firms use new ideas to solve new problems quickly.
options.C: They mainly make firms follow fixed routines for better efficiency.
options.D: They let one person work alone on every task.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words about workers, ideas, firms.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Knowledge and intangible assets become the principal sources of value, reducing reliance on physical resources.
Concept 2: Human capital and specialized, adaptable skills are the core drivers of economic development in a knowledge economy.
Concept 3: Intellectual property and information assets play a central role in growth, linking the knowledge economy to the information/digital economy and Information Age.

questions.question: How do rights that belong to everyone fit with local customs? What law or group helps settle this conflict?

options.A: Universality says one set of rules applies everywhere; cultural relativism rejects that.
options.B: They are the same idea; no law tool is needed.
options.C: Treaty law protects core rights from customs; UN monitors help enforce those rights.
options.D: Cultural customs override universal rights; nothing enforces them.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified words for children. I kept the main idea about universal rights versus local customs.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Universal and inalienable nature of human rights as a guiding principle and baseline for equality.
Concept 2: Mechanisms of protection and promotion (national/international law, the UDHR as a framework, treaties, and monitoring institutions like the United Nations and NGOs).
Concept 3: Relationships and tensions between universality and cultural/contextual variation (cultural relativism, implementation across cultures, and the historical shift after WWII).

questions.question: Why must we treat information fairly at every step of its life?

options.A: It says making information ends ethical duties.
options.B: It says only privacy matters, not access or use rights.
options.C: It says rights and duties last through the whole life of information.
options.D: It says only librarians must follow ethics, not others.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened words and used simple sentences to show lifecycle duties and fair sharing.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Information lifecycle ethics and rights (creation, collection, recording, distribution, processing; ownership, copyright, digital divide, digital rights) and the ethical obligations they entail.
Concept 2: Moral agency in the infosphere (whether artificial agents can be moral and how that possibility affects information ethics).

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary relationships and frameworks (how information ethics relates to computer ethics, medical ethics, journalism, AI, big data ethics, and the role of information professionals in disseminating information responsibly).

questions.question: Why should leaders learn facts, skills, and goodness together?

options.A: Facts, skills, and goodness help leaders make fair trusted decisions.
options.B: Facts alone are enough for good leadership.
options.C: Goodness alone guides leaders without facts or skills.
options.D: Charisma alone makes leaders succeed without facts or goodness.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to keep More's idea of facts, skills, and goodness.
concepts_tested: The triad of science, art, and personal virtue as elements of effective statesmanship (More’s perspective)
Narrow vs. broad conceptions of statecraft: managing interstate relations for national advantage vs. studying states and governance more broadly
Statecraft as a contested, interdisciplinary concept linking theory (philosophical ideas) and practice (public diplomacy, governance, leadership)

questions.question: Why do rules of politics push change through talks and laws, not by force?

options.A: Rules make changes legal and stable, increasing how much people accept them.
options.B: Force is always illegal and cannot fairly move resources.
options.C: Talks and laws always give the best result for every person.
options.D: Elections alone automatically move resources and end other methods.

questions.simplification_notes: Rewrote the idea using simple words and short sentences for kids.
concepts_tested: Politics as power relations and collective decision-making that determine the distribution of resources and status
Political systems as frameworks that define acceptable methods and constrain or enable political action
Mechanisms of political action (negotiation, lawmaking, party organization, elections, use of force) and how they interact to produce political change

questions.question: Why should a country link defense, trade, and aid in its foreign plans?

options.A: Linking defense, trade, and aid makes a country's foreign policy stronger.
options.B: Defense and trade conflict with aid, forcing leaders to pick one.
options.C: Each goal is separate, letting leaders focus on one at a time.
options.D: Giving aid can make a country rely on others, harming its safety.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to make a short clear question.
concepts_tested: Interconnectedness of objectives: defense, economic interests, and humanitarian aid are not isolated but contribute to a single, comprehensive foreign policy; test with how/why these goals influence one another.

Factors shaping policy decisions: domestic considerations, behavior of other states, and geopolitical strategies affect how foreign policy is formulated; test with why these factors matter and how they interact.

Institutional mechanisms in policy formation: diplomatic corps and think tanks serve as instruments to develop, advocate, and inform foreign policy decisions; test with how these institutions influence policy outcomes.

questions.question: Why must goals match what you can do when things are unsure?

options.A: Matching goals to resources helps you change plans when things change.
options.B: Alignment always makes resources fit a fixed goal without change.
options.C: Misalignment only matters when resources are plenty.
options.D: The goal-means link is unimportant after you make a plan.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified words to make the idea easy for young students.
concepts_tested: Ends-means alignment: how goals, priorities, and available resources are connected and trade-offs made under conditions of uncertainty.
Emergent vs deliberate strategy: strategy as a planned set of actions versus a pattern that emerges from ongoing decisions and environmental adaptation.
Policy-to-action relationship in military strategy: how political objectives shape and constrain military means and actions, and the tension between ends (policy) and means (military capability).

questions.question: How does thinking about big ideas plus making step by step plans help reach goals?

options.A: Big idea thinking produces fixed actions for planning to change.
options.B: Planning makes ideas from big-picture thinking into steps with time plus people.
options.C: Planning makes new options and scenarios for thinking to choose.
options.D: Thinking equals planning, making one replaceable.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to show how thinking and planning work together.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Strategic thinking prioritizes asks "Why" and "How" over "What," shaping decisions through reasoning, analysis, and synthesis rather than mere description.
Concept 2: Strategic thinking and strategic planning are distinct but complementary processes; both are needed and interact to improve strategy and execution.
Concept 3: Foresight and collaborative group thinking expand possibility space and generate diverse perspectives, contributing to proactive dialogue and competitive advantage.

questions.question: Why do math models help leaders make better choices than guessing?

options.A: It always finds the perfect choice even if the model is wrong.
options.B: It turns goals, limits, trade-offs, and tests of changing numbers into a clear model.
options.C: It removes all doubt about the future.
options.D: It replaces all human choices with automatic, fair machine choices.

questions.simplification_notes: I removed hard words and used short, clear sentences with simple examples.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Quantitative modeling and optimization as tools to illuminate management issues and achieve optimal or near-optimal decisions.
Concept 2: A three-level framework (fundamental mathematics; modeling; application) that structures management science research and practice.
Concept 3: Interdisciplinary and cross-domain applicability, illustrating how rational, systematic techniques transfer from business to military, medical, public administration, and other contexts.

questions.question: How does a cycle for information make it more useful for the right people?

options.A: It treats each stage alone without looking at later needs.
options.B: It creates feedback from disposal to earlier steps to improve quality and relevance.
options.C: It makes storage cost the only reason for decisions.
options.D: It delays disposal to extend data retention regardless of utility.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one simple sentence using plain words.
concepts_tested: The information management lifecycle: acquisition, custodianship/storage, distribution, and disposal as an integrated cycle.
Governance and stakeholder roles: quality, accessibility, utility, rights to originate/change/distribute/delete information, and policy-driven control.
Relational scope and value creation: IM overlaps with data, systems, technology, processes, and organizational strategy, and information gains value when it is effectively used by the right audience.

questions.question: Why do leaders make big plans while managers turn plans into teamwork?

options.A: Leaders turn big plans directly into daily tasks for frontline workers.
options.B: Managers set big goals while they watch outcomes.
options.C: Leaders set big plans while managers turn plans into actions using feedback.
options.D: Leaders and managers do the same jobs.

questions.simplification_notes: Swapped big words for leaders, managers, big plans, and teamwork.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Hierarchical structure of management and the flow of strategic goals from senior to line management (how goals/policies are translated into frontline actions).
Concept 2: Distinction between governance and management (what senior management does in setting strategy/policy versus directing and implementing through subordinates).
Concept 3: Management as an interdisciplinary, practice-informed field (the relationship between theory, education, and evidence-based management).

questions.question: Why do group rules help people know how to act? How do those rules stay the same over time?

options.A: People copy rewarded actions.
options.B: Laws force people to act the same.
options.C: Genes make people follow group rules.
options.D: Norms only describe what people do.

questions.simplification_notes: Simpler words kept the social learning idea.
concepts_tested: Cultural transmission through social learning (enculturation and socialization) as the mechanism by which individuals acquire culture
Norms as templates for acceptable conduct and social expectations that shape behavior
Cultural change dynamics: internal forces and external contact leading to repositioning or transformation of culture

questions.question: How does testing quick models with people help a team make a better solution?

options.A: Each test proves the first idea early.
options.B: Each test shows wrong guesses, gets people's feedback, and lets teams reframe the problem.
options.C: Prototypes speed final production by putting all needs into the first model.
options.D: Iteration locks teams into one path.

questions.simplification_notes: Made wording simple and focused on testing models and learning.
concepts_tested: Wicked problems and design thinking: why design thinking is suited to ill-defined/wicked problems and how it differs from solving well-defined problems.
Iterative, non-linear process with prototyping and user testing: how iteration drives learning and solution development.
Abductive and productive reasoning as core cognitive mechanisms: how these reasoning modes contribute to generating creative, viable design solutions.

questions.question: How does knowing how users think help a designer test a design?

options.A: It gives ideas to test about what users want by watching people try tasks.
options.B: It makes the designer focus only on how things look.
options.C: It stops designers from testing designs with real users.
options.D: It makes designers trust only their own ideas.

questions.simplification_notes: Made language simple, kept testing idea by watching users.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Research and data drive design decisions in UX design, rather than relying on aesthetic preferences.
Concept 2: UX design is holistic, covering all aspects of the user’s perceived experience (usability, usefulness, desirability, brand perception, performance) and not limited to the interface.

Concept 3: UX design draws on multiple disciplines (HCI, UCD, IA, visual design, etc.) and relies on empathy and understanding of users’ needs and mental models to shape and validate design decisions.

questions.question: Why must designers pick what to fix first when they make things for everyone?

options.A: Designers think one design can help every person with endless money.
options.B: Designers must pick fixes that help the most people when money is short.
options.C: Designers may skip small rare fixes to make things easier for most people.
options.D: Designers let only their own opinion decide the changes.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified wording for kids.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Universal design aims to maximize usability for the greatest number of people by removing barriers and enabling participation.
Concept 2: Inclusion-oriented design involves trade-offs and prioritization, recognizing dilemmas when designing for diverse needs.

Concept 3: Universal design emerges from barrier-free design and accessibility movements and requires interdisciplinary collaboration (engineering, architecture, medicine) with socio-material implications.

questions.question: Why must designers build clear structure for help tools to read a product? Why does this still matter when people can use the product easily?

options.A: Indirect access only means adding extra content after a product exists.
options.B: Help tools need clear structure to understand content reliably.
options.C: Only using large text or strong color contrast gives indirect access.
options.D: Indirect access only serves legal rules, not user experience.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed technical terms, used simple words, kept ideas short.
concepts_tested: Direct vs indirect access: the mechanisms by which accessibility enables unassisted use and compatibility with assistive technology.
Relationships among accessibility, usability, and universal design: how these concepts interrelate, differ in emphasis, and collectively guide design goals.
Rights-based, societal rationale for accessibility: the legislative framework and goal of enabling participation to close digital/knowledge divides.

questions.question: How do schools and media help the main culture feel normal to many people?

options.A: People naturally think the same way.
options.B: The state forces one meaning on people with punishments and laws.
options.C: A network of schools, TV, religions, and courts spreads ideas tied to power.
options.D: Global technology makes cultures very similar everywhere.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I named schools, TV, laws, and churches as examples.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Culture as a power-laden, meaning-generating process (how cultural practices relate to ideology, class, nation, and other social forces; how meaning is produced, disseminated, and contested).
Concept 2: Culture as dynamic and processual (cultures are interacting, changing sets of practices rather than fixed entities).

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary frameworks and mechanisms (use of multiple theoretical approaches including Marxism, semiotics, feminist theory, postcolonialism; ideas like cultural hegemony and agency; globalization as a context for cultural analysis).

questions.question: How can a belief make your body act if it is not only brain parts?

options.A: Because beliefs are the same as brain states.
options.B: Because beliefs depend on brain states to play causal roles.
options.C: Because only physical processes cause actions, not beliefs.
options.D: Because a separate mind moves the body.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words in short sentences.
concepts_tested: The mind–body relationship: distinctness vs. unity of mind and body and whether they can causally interact
Competing theoretical frameworks: dualism, physicalism/monism, idealism, functionalism, property dualism, non-reductive theories
Mental causation and agency: how mental states (beliefs, desires) causally influence physical actions and implications for free will and moral responsibility

questions.question: How does a person's identity help them choose actions that keep them well?

options.A: It forces people to follow fixed roles.
options.B: It links values, goals, roles to guide choices, coping and well-being.
options.C: Genes alone make identity that never changes.
options.D: It is only a label that does not change actions.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to show identity as a guide for choices and well-being.
concepts_tested: Identity as a self-regulatory structure that provides meaning, direction, and behavioral guidance, affecting adaptation and well-being.
The relationship between personal identity and collective identity, and how social/cultural factors and others’ perceptions shape both individual behavior and group membership expectations.
Developmental and contextual dynamics of identity: emergence in childhood, stability yet potential for change, and situational adaptability, as explained by theoretical frameworks.

questions.question: Why do instincts come from genes? How does selection act more on ability to learn than on one learned act?

options.A: Instincts stay fixed across species; they change only by genes; learning has no value.
options.B: Genes make instincts helping survival; learning comes from experience; selection acts on learning ability.
options.C: Instincts come from social learning, not from genes; learned acts come from genes.
options.D: Both instincts and learned acts come equally from the environment; selection acts on each.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to remove technical terms.
concepts_tested: Ethology applies an evolutionary/natural selection framework to understanding behavior (including instinctive vs. other behavior).
Ethograms as a foundational methodological tool for describing and quantifying behavior.
Ethology as an interdisciplinary enterprise that combines laboratory and field study and connects with neuroanatomy, ecology, and evolutionary biology.

questions.question: Why do we rank risks before choosing ways to lower them?

options.A: We focus on risks with biggest chance of happening or biggest harm.
options.B: Evaluating risks makes us treat all risks the same.
options.C: Prioritization lets you delay fixing big risks until you have more resources.
options.D: After we prioritize risks, monitoring becomes unnecessary.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified language for children.
concepts_tested: The risk management process: identification, evaluation, prioritization, followed by minimization, monitoring, and control of risks.
Risk treatment strategies and the risk–opportunity relationship: avoidance, reduction, transfer, and retention for threats, with opposite considerations for opportunities.
Standards and professional roles shaping practice: influence of organizations (PMI, NIST, ISO, actuarial societies) and roles like risk manager and risk analyst across contexts.

questions.question: If risk means uncertainty changing goals, why can it be good or bad? What should managers do about it?

options.A: Managers should only try to avoid harm.
options.B: Managers should manage chances as well as threats to goals.
options.C: Managers should only try to reduce possible losses.
options.D: Managers should ignore risk.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and split the question into two short sentences.
concepts_tested: Risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (deviation from expected; can be positive or negative)
Components of risk (risk sources, potential events, their consequences, and their likelihood)
Cross-domain application of risk management principles (ISO 31000 guidance) and the idea that risk management involves principles and processes, not just definitions

questions.question: Why do safety messages that match a group's values make people act?

options.A: It makes the message shorter.
options.B: Matching values increases action.
options.C: It shows only true facts.
options.D: It uses fear to make people act.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Distinction between risk communication and crisis communication (goals, scope, outcomes)
Concept 2: Mechanisms of risk communication (tailoring messages to audience values, raising awareness, persuading behavior change to reduce threats)
Concept 3: Contexts and scales (community-level vs. individual-level risk communication; influence of information sources and decision-making processes)

questions.question: Why does proving the cause came before the effect matter? Why does removing other reasons help show a real cause?

options.A: An earlier cause with other reasons removed makes a likely true cause.
options.B: A large sample size alone makes a link become a cause.
options.C: Adjusting for one reason proves the exposure caused the disease.
options.D: Biological fit alone proves a cause without good study design.

questions.simplification_notes: I used short questions and simple words to show time order and removing other reasons.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The relationship between distribution/ppatterns/determinants in a population and the use of that knowledge to prevent disease.
Concept 2: Causation vs correlation in epidemiology, including how study design, data collection, and statistical analysis identify risk factors and assess treatment effects.

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary integration (biology, statistics, social sciences, engineering) and the broad application of epidemiology beyond infectious diseases to chronic conditions and population-level health outcomes.

questions.question: Why giving people power to change housing and work helps health more than teaching?

options.A: It mainly gives people better access to health services.
options.B: It lets people change housing, income, and work conditions to make healthier choices.
options.C: It only runs quick education programs.
options.D: It claims genes decide health outcomes.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words with housing, income, and work examples.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Health promotion as enabling/empowering people to influence health determinants (mechanism linking empowerment to health outcomes)
Concept 2: The centrality of social determinants (income, housing, employment, etc.) and multi-sectoral policy approaches (Health in All Policies) in producing health outcomes
Concept 3: Distinction between health promotion and health education, and the idea that preventive, environmental, and policy interventions are core to promotion (not just individual behavior change)

questions.question: How do three rules for finding causes work together? What problem does each rule fix?

options.A: Things changing together alone prove one thing caused the other.
options.B: Time order stops reverse cause; removing causes stops hidden factors; change together supports cause.
options.C: Time order checks size only; removing others helps measurement only; change together decides cause.
options.D: These rules apply only in random experiments; observational data gives no help.

questions.simplification_notes: Changed hard words to simple words. Kept the three rules, their linked problems.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Causality requires observing the effect when the cause is changed (distinction from mere association)
Concept 2: Criteria for inferring causality: covariation, time-order (cause precedes effect), and elimination of plausible alternative causes
Concept 3: Role of methodological testing (falsifiable null hypotheses and statistical frameworks like frequentist vs Bayesian) in supporting causal claims

questions.question: How does random assignment help researchers know a treatment caused a change? How do p-values test if seen differences are unlikely by chance?

options.A: It makes measurements more precise, lowering p-values.
options.B: It guarantees treatment effects, making p-values always show real effects.
options.C: Random assignment balances other factors across groups, allowing p-values to test treatment effects.
options.D: It removes all experimental errors, making p-values exact.

questions.simplification_notes: Made two short questions. Used simple words for children.
concepts_tested: The design of experiments and statistical inference as foundational principles for how biological data are collected, analyzed, and interpreted.
The integration of statistics with genetics and evolution to form population genetics and the modern evolutionary synthesis (conceptual link between disciplines).
The use of foundational statistical tools (e.g., ANOVA, p-values, Fisher’s exact test) as mechanisms enabling testing and interpretation of biological hypotheses.

questions.question: Why do policies that change where people live cut unfair health gaps more?

options.A: Only changing habits helps individuals more than it helps the whole group.
options.B: Social conditions do not affect people's health.
options.C: Changing social conditions only makes hospitals treat sick people better.
options.D: Habit programs take resources away from poor communities.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced technical words with simple words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Social determinants of health are major determinants of population health outcomes and health inequities.
Concept 2: Population health shifts focus from individual-level factors to population-level determinants and policies/interventions to improve health.
Concept 3: Health is a broader construct than absence of disease, including capacity to adapt to life’s challenges and overall well-being.

questions.question: Why does making media in one place let it reach many people far away? Why does it make quick feedback and local tailoring hard?

options.A: It lets every viewer give real-time personal feedback to change content quickly.
options.B: It separates makers and viewers, giving messages wide reach and low feedback.
options.C: It forces local creators to produce content for their town.
options.D: It sends content instantly with no lag.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I split cause and effect into two short questions.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Production and distribution are both technical and institutional processes that shape how mass media outputs are created and circulated.
Concept 2: Commodification of symbolic forms drives content production and dissemination by tying meaning and imagery to economic value.
Concept 3: The mass media function as a one-to-many, time/space-spanning system with a separation between where content is produced and where/how audiences receive it.

questions.question: How do back-end pay deals link pay to profits? Why does this need exact accounting rules that show who gets paid first?

options.A: It promises profits to participants even without accounting checks.
options.B: Linking pay to profits aligns incentives while requiring exact accounting rules, payment order.
options.C: It moves all risk to financiers, leaving accounting unnecessary.
options.D: It has no impact on incentives because it is a marketing term.

questions.simplification_notes: Made wording simple and split ideas into two short questions for clarity.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Interdisciplinary nature of entertainment law and its overlap with multiple legal domains (IP, contract, labor, First Amendment, etc.), requiring cross-field understanding.
Concept 2: Transaction-based practice and the negotiation/drafting mechanism (contracts, compensation, profit participation) that drive most work in the field, with potential for dispute resolution.
Concept 3: IP framework as foundational to entertainment work (copyright ownership, exclusive rights) and the role of institutions like the US Copyright Office in establishing and recording rights.

questions.question: How does a person's past change what they see in a picture?

options.A: Everyone sees the same meaning from pictures.
options.B: Seeing uses only basic things like color and light.
options.C: The brain uses past knowledge to make meaning from unclear pictures.
options.D: Only culture decides what pictures mean.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified words and used short sentences for third graders.
concepts_tested: Perception-based interpretation: how the brain constructs meaning from visual imagery and how interpretation varies with the viewer’s experience.
Design element framework: the seven components (color, shape, tones, texture, figure-ground, balance, hierarchy) as core levers shaping visual communication.
Evaluation principle: effectiveness is judged by audience comprehension rather than aesthetic preferences.

questions.question: How does mixing markets with some government help use scarce resources well?

options.A: One group sets all prices.
options.B: It uses only prices and private contracts.
options.C: It mixes markets with government fixes.
options.D: It removes price signals.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I kept the markets and government idea.
concepts_tested: The four fundamental economic problems (what to produce, how to produce, for whom to produce/distribute, when to produce) and how pricing and resource constraints influence these decisions.
The role of institutions, information flows, and property rights in coordinating production and allocation within an economic system.
The evolution from market/plan dichotomies to market-oriented mixed economies and why the chosen system matters for incentives and resource allocation.

questions.question: How does a set of ideas hold together when new facts clash?

options.A: It lets new facts replace old ideas.
options.B: It uses old boxes to sort new facts.
options.C: It treats every new fact as true.
options.D: It waits for outside proof before changing any belief.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I used a box metaphor.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Ideology as a "science of ideas" linking sensory experience to ideas to form a structured system of thought (mechanism/principle).
Concept 2: The balance between practical elements and theoretical elements within an ideology (relationship between practice and theory).
Concept 3: Normative core of ideology (defense of individual liberty, property, free markets, and constitutional limits on state power).

questions.question: When a factory pollutes others, how does a tax equal to the harm help?

options.A: The tax raises private cost to match society's cost, raising total welfare.
options.B: It taxes all production costs, giving the money to the government.
options.C: It exempts polluters from taxes, increasing production.
options.D: It makes consumers pay through price rules, leaving firms unchanged.

questions.simplification_notes: Changed hard words to simple words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Market failures justify government intervention (e.g., public goods, externalities, imperfect competition) and the mechanisms by which policy addresses them.
Concept 2: Balancing efficiency and equity in policy design using welfare economics and normative analysis to guide tax and expenditure decisions.
Concept 3: Distinction between market failure and government failure, plus the framework for deciding the appropriate extent of government participation in the economy.

questions.question: Why would a person pay to learn some things earlier?

options.A: The value of information equals the extra benefit of choosing after knowing things earlier.
