options.B: The value of information measures the average gain from one extra decision.
options.C: Moving unknowns earlier never changes the best decision.
options.D: The value of information depends only on how many unknowns there are.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words with short sentences.
concepts_tested: The value of information depends on when information is available in the decision sequence; moving uncertainties earlier increases the potential payoff and is captured by the highest price a decision-maker would pay (VoI as the price for reordering information).
VoI is connected to other information values and can be seen as the value of perfect information on some related uncertainty (VoC), tying together VOI with EVPI and EVSI.

The standard decision framework assumes rationality with perfect recall and a linear ordering of decisions and uncertainties; this ordering defines what is known when decisions are made and how VOI is computed.

questions.question: Why does more information make the numbers we find less spread out?

options.A: More information lowers bias in the estimates.
options.B: A basic bound says variance is at least the inverse of information.
options.C: The information matrix equals the estimator variance.
options.D: More information lowers the number of things to find.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed math jargon to use simple words about information and spread.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The connection between estimator variance and information
Concept 2: Model- and criterion-dependent nature of optimal designs
Concept 3: A-optimality as a concrete criterion

questions.question: How does thinking about your thinking help when tasks switch from new to practiced?

options.A: It always helps performance by making you pay attention to everything.
options.B: It shifts control to conscious thinking to make routines faster.
options.C: It helps new problems by using careful thinking while it hurts practiced tasks.
options.D: It only affects memory not perception or movement.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard words with simple phrases about thinking about thinking.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Functional organization of cognitive processes (perception, attention, memory, thinking, language) and how they interact to acquire, store, transform, and use information.

Concept 2: Competing theories/models of cognition (classical computationalism, connectionism) and the role of representations (representationalism vs. anti-representationalism) in explaining processing mechanisms.
Concept 3: Metacognition and the distinction between conscious and unconscious, as well as controlled and automatic processes, including how these regulate and monitor other mental processes.

questions.question: Why does Plato say justice means giving people jobs they do best? What happens if someone takes a job they cannot do?

options.A: It makes outcomes equal for everyone.
options.B: Matching jobs to skills keeps the city working; mismatched roles break its balance.
options.C: Justice needs punishment for people who do not do their job.
options.D: Divine commands make justice; authority must fix any change.

questions.simplification_notes: I shortened the question. I used simple words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Multiple justice frameworks (distributive, utilitarian, retributive, restorative) and how each justifies outcomes (distribution of goods, punishment, or repair of harms)
Concept 2: Foundational grounds for justice (natural law, social contract, divine command) and how these explain why justice should govern conduct
Concept 3: Platonic justice as balance and proper assignment of roles within a community, with fairness based on contribution and proportionate reciprocation

questions.question: Two firms must buy special tools for each other's work. How does joining the firms change what they do later?

options.A: It always makes the whole process the cheapest.
options.B: It joins the firms to stop cheating after they buy special tools.
options.C: It makes all hidden information vanish.
options.D: It makes the market always competitive.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and a short story about two firms. I kept the idea that joining firms stops cheating.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Boundaries between firms and markets are driven by real-world frictions (transaction costs, imperfect information, barriers to entry), which explain deviations from perfect competition.

Concept 2: Strategic interaction and market power are analyzed through models (notably game theory) and empirical analysis to explain oligopoly behavior, pricing strategies, vertical integration, and contracting.
Concept 3: Public policy and regulation shape market organization and incentives (antitrust policy, competition policy, governance of law, property rights, contracts).

questions.question: How do receptive fields help the brain find where the skin is touched?

options.A: Larger receptive fields activate more neurons.
options.B: Larger receptive fields give more precise signals.
options.C: Smaller receptive fields cause different firing in nearby receptors.
options.D: Receptive field size only changes sensitivity.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified words. Kept idea that small fields give finer touch location.
concepts_tested: Receptive fields and their role in sensory perception
Receptor transduction and the neural pathway (stimulus -> receptor -> action potential -> brain processing)
Modality-specific receptors and their signal routes (chemoreceptors, photoreceptors, mechanoreceptors, thermoreceptors) and how they link stimuli to perception

questions.question: How do the middle ear bones help move sound into the inner ear fluid?

options.A: The ossicles vibrate at a higher frequency than the incoming sound.
options.B: The ossicles act as a lever to focus sound on the small oval window.
options.C: Ear muscles make signals to amplify the sound.
options.D: The round window reflects most sound back into the middle ear.

questions.simplification_notes: I used easier words. I showed lever action with a small window.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The mechanical-to-neural transduction pathway in the ear (tympanic membrane → ossicles → oval window → cochlea → organ of Corti → neural signals).
Concept 2: Impedance matching by the middle ear ossicles to convert airborne sound waves into fluid motions in the cochlea efficiently.
Concept 3: Roles of ear structure and reflexes in sound localization and protection (outer ear localization cues due to asymmetry; stapedius/tensor tympani reflexes shielding the inner ear).

questions.question: Why do big social changes need a steady background plus sudden events?

options.A: Only a steady background decides change.
options.B: Only sudden events decide change.
options.C: Steady background makes weak spots and chances for events to trigger.
options.D: Social change only repeats in cycles.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words. Kept idea of steady background plus sudden events.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Social change results from a combination of systematic (structural) factors and unique (contingent/random) factors, implying an interaction between stable conditions and variables.
Concept 2: Theories of social change identify core components—structural aspects, processes/mechanisms, and directions of change—as a framework for explaining how change happens.

Concept 3: Different theoretical frameworks propose distinct mechanisms for change (e.g., Hegelian dialectic with conflict and synthesis; Marxist class struggle and materialist history; Kuhnian paradigm shifts and critical junctures), highlighting how the underlying drivers differ across theories.

questions.question: Why do people use what they buy to show who they are?

options.A: People only try to pay the lowest price.
options.B: People buy brands and items to show group, status, and values.
options.C: Buying things makes everyone like the same things.
options.D: People form their identity only from work.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and short sentences for children.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Consumer sovereignty and its influence on production and economic organization (how consumer preferences shape what is produced and how).
Concept 2: Consumption as a cultural and identity-forming activity (how buying and consuming contribute to social meaning and status, beyond basic needs).
Concept 3: Environmental and social externalities of consumerism (overconsumption, resource depletion, waste, climate change, and inequality).

questions.question: How do many small changes make a system become a new kind?

options.A: Many small changes add up to make the system a new kind.
options.B: Qualities do not depend on amounts.
options.C: Qualitative change always follows quantity smoothly.
options.D: The new quality decides the amount of change.

questions.simplification_notes: This version uses simple words. It keeps the main idea about small changes making new qualities.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Unity and conflict of opposites (contradictions within systems drive change)
Concept 2: Quantity into quality (small quantitative changes accumulating to a qualitative transformation)
Concept 3: Negation of the negation (development through iterative negation leading to higher forms or arrangements)

questions.question: How do gut feelings and slow careful thinking help experts solve hard problems?

options.A: Gut feeling gives quick answers.
options.B: Gut feeling and slow careful thinking provide quick ideas and careful checks.
options.C: They work exactly the same.
options.D: Slow careful thinking finds detailed steps.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced complex terms with 'gut feeling' and 'slow careful thinking'.
concepts_tested: Distinction and relationship between logical discursive reasoning and intuitive reasoning (how they differ, clash, and may complement each other)
Forms of logical reasoning (deductive, inductive, abductive) as mechanisms for deriving conclusions
Reasoning as a cognitive mechanism linked to decision making, knowledge formation, and cultural/disciplinary contexts, including its study in psychology, cognitive science, and automated reasoning

questions.question: Which statement shows that tools and society change each other over time?

options.A: People change to match tools.
options.B: Institutions control tool design.
options.C: Tools and people change each other over time.
options.D: Technology grows without people shaping it.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words to show that tools and people shape each other.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mutual shaping/coevolution of technology and society (technology and social structures influence each other over time).
Concept 2: Technology as a driver of social/economic transformation and values (how tools enable changes in communication, production, economy, and ethics).
Concept 3: Frameworks and governance as mediators of tech–society outcomes (the role of theories like STS, tektology, and institutional context in shaping impact).

questions.question: Why does a new tool spread differently in different places?

options.A: Everyone uses a working tool the same way everywhere.
options.B: People, rules, and money shape how people make and use a tool.
options.C: Only the first design choices of engineers decide how people use a tool.
options.D: Laws always make people use a new tool faster everywhere.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words for children. Kept idea that people, rules, and money shape how tools spread.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Science and technology are socially embedded and studied within historical, cultural, and social contexts.
Concept 2: Technological and scientific development is not determined by technology alone; it is shaped by social factors and contested through contextual critiques (e.g., anti-determinism).

Concept 3: Interdisciplinary integration and institutional formation of STS (collaboration across disciplines and the development of programs/centers), including examination of power dynamics within science and society.

questions.question: How do rules and ethics help people make new tools without causing harm?

options.A: They make rules that keep technology useful without causing harm.
options.B: They do not matter.
options.C: They only slow progress without fixing harms.
options.D: They stop all new technology, removing benefits and harms.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words to keep the main idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Technology as the systematic application of knowledge to practical ends (the link between conceptual knowledge and productive tools)
Concept 2: Technology as a driver of social and economic change, with both positive outcomes (prosperity, communication) and negative impacts (pollution, unemployment)
Concept 3: The ethical and political dimensions of technology, including debates about its role, governance, and ways to mitigate downsides

questions.question: How can rules stay the same and also slowly change?

options.A: Rules stay the same because people cannot change them.
options.B: People learn rules and can create new rules that slowly change society.
options.C: Only leaders make new laws that force people to change.
options.D: Rules come only from biology and cannot change with culture.

questions.simplification_notes: Made words simple for kids and kept the idea that people shape rules and rules shape people.
concepts_tested: Societal norms as mechanisms that regulate behavior and enable coordination within a group.
The bidirectional relationship between individuals and society: humans shape society, and society shapes humans (co-construction).
Technology and economic activity shaping social structure and stratification (how surplus and economic organization influence class, governance, and institutions).

questions.question: Why do catalysts work best when molecules stick the right amount?

options.A: Moderate adsorption balances activation with easy desorption.
options.B: Gas amount controls turnover.
options.C: Stronger adsorption always speeds the reaction.
options.D: Both weak and strong sticking affect the rate equally.

questions.simplification_notes: I used 'stick' for adsorption to keep the optimal sticking idea.
concepts_tested: Adsorption mechanisms and their effect on catalysis (chemisorption vs physisorption; Sabatier principle)
Model vs. real catalysts: using single-crystal surfaces to study surface interactions and structure–activity relationships
Langmuir adsorption model: assumptions about monolayer coverage, equal affinity, and no adsorbate interactions, and its role as a framework and its limitations

questions.question: Why do prices in good markets stop trades that help one without hurting another?

options.A: Prices match buyers' values to sellers' costs.
options.B: A planner gives resources to try to help everyone most.
options.C: Auctions always give the best outcome.
options.D: Any allocation that uses all resources is always best for everyone.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard words with simple ones. Explained prices match buyers to sellers.
concepts_tested: Pareto efficiency as a criterion for evaluating resource allocation outcomes and the conditions under which mechanisms achieve it
Mechanisms of allocation (markets, planning, auctions, algorithmic allocation) and how they influence distribution of resources
Contingency and prioritization rules (priority rankings, funding trade-offs) used to manage scarcity and guide allocation decisions

questions.question: Why do people have only so much attention? How does this change doing many tasks at once?

options.A: People can increase attention without limit.
options.B: Using attention on one task reduces attention for another.
options.C: Attention spreads equally among all inputs.
options.D: Attention acts only on noticeable things.

questions.simplification_notes: I made words simple for kids. I kept the correct answer as B.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Attention as a limited cognitive resource with finite processing capacity, affecting how we allocate mental effort across tasks.
Concept 2: Economic framing of attention, where time and attention are valued and analyzed with economic theory (e.g., attention as a commodity, value of free goods).
Concept 3: Design and information-management mechanisms (filtering/out filtering of information) driven by information overload, shaping how systems should allocate and prioritize attention.

questions.question: How do internet tools help companies make, move, and sell things better?

options.A: They raise entry costs for new firms.
options.B: They let companies collect data fast and coordinate to make goods when customers want.
options.C: They make companies work alone and slow new ideas.
options.D: They change transactions to paper to make them more reliable.

questions.simplification_notes: I removed hard words and used simple data and teamwork examples.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Digital transformation mechanism — how ICTs and Internet/Web technologies reconfigure production, distribution, and trade, altering business models and productivity.
Concept 2: The e-business/e-commerce framework — the interrelated components (infrastructure, e-business processes, and online commerce) that together enable digital economic activity.
Concept 3: Governance and regulatory relationships — how the digital economy creates new challenges and needs for privacy, competition, and taxation regulation between states, firms, and individuals.

questions.question: How can the same genes make different traits when the surroundings change?

options.A: The surroundings change which genes cells use to make traits.
options.B: The surroundings change the DNA code to make new traits.
options.C: The surroundings change proteins after cells make them.
options.D: The surroundings change the working copy of a gene.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced technical terms with simple words covering genes, surroundings.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Genes as discrete units of inheritance that transmit traits across generations (genotype-phenotype relationship and Mendelian inheritance)
Concept 2: Gene expression and phenotype are influenced by the environment, illustrating gene-environment interaction and transcriptional regulation
Concept 3: Genetic variation and analysis across multiple levels (molecular, cellular, organismal, population) and across subfields (molecular genetics, epigenetics, population genetics)

questions.question: Why does the root location change the most recent common ancestor of some leaves?

options.A: Moving the root can change which node is the most recent common ancestor.
options.B: Distances between leaves fix the shared ancestor.
options.C: The shared ancestor is always the node next to the root for any subset.
options.D: Changing the root only changes branch order.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened the question for third graders. Kept the idea that root placement can change the MRCA.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Phylogenetic trees encode evolutionary relationships and common ancestry; rooted trees establish direction and identify the most recent common ancestor (MRCA).
Concept 2: Internal nodes represent hypothetical ancestors and edge lengths can reflect divergence times, enabling temporal interpretations of evolution.
Concept 3: Computational phylogenetics uses algorithms to infer an optimal tree, highlighting principles of modeling, data usage (genetic/phenotypic), and methodological choices.

questions.question: Why does putting a root on a species tree show which changes came first?

options.A: Because the root marks the oldest ancestor.
options.B: Because rooting changes the data.
options.C: Because rooting gives exact ages for every split.
options.D: Because rooting tells the exact trait at every ancestor.

questions.simplification_notes: Short wording for kids. Used 'root' to mean the oldest ancestor showing order.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Phylogenetics infers evolutionary relationships from observable, heritable data (DNA, proteins, morphology) and represents them in a phylogenetic tree.
Concept 2: Rooted vs unrooted trees convey different information about ancestry and the direction of character state changes.
Concept 3: Phylogenetics has broad practical applications (e.g., cancer clonal evolution, drug discovery, forensic science), illustrating how phylogenetic reasoning yields biological insights.

questions.question: Why do many cultures use a tree to link heaven, earth, and the underworld?

options.A: People think one real tree species grows everywhere, linking all realms.
options.B: A vertical tree image maps realms, reflects shared human thinking.
options.C: People use the tree only as wall decoration, not for cosmology.
options.D: Some think the tree is an old plant diagram later read as myth.

questions.simplification_notes: Made words simple, kept idea of tree linking realms with shared use.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The tree of life as a world tree/axis mundi that connects heavens, earth, and the underworld, with cross-cultural parallels (e.g., Yggdrasil, Epic of Gilgamesh’s immortality quest).
Concept 2: The tree of life as an archetype representing immortality and fertility, recurring in myth and religious symbolism.
Concept 3: The relationship between symbolic imagery and religious practice, including continuity and adaptation of symbols (Asherah, temple iconography, and the menorah) across cultures.

questions.question: Why does storing group knowledge in routines and tools help when staff leave?

options.A: It stores knowledge in shared routines and tools for the whole group.
options.B: It makes private skills written down for everyone.
options.C: It keeps most knowledge only with a few experts.
options.D: It stops the group from changing methods.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and a clear example about routines keeping knowledge when people leave.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Knowledge creation, retention, and transfer as adaptive processes driven by organizational experience.

Concept 2: The learning curve and organizational learning rates as a relationship between cumulative production and productivity, influenced by factors such as individual proficiency, technology, and coordination structures.

Concept 3: The distribution of knowledge between individuals and the organization, and the importance of embedding and transferring knowledge for sustained organizational learning (including risks when individuals withhold knowledge or leave).

questions.question: Why can crossing an Earth limit cause sudden big changes?

options.A: Earth parts push each other to make small changes become big ones.
options.B: Boundaries are fixed lines with only small steady effects.
options.C: Sudden shocks from space or volcanoes cause abrupt changes.
options.D: Crossing one boundary makes every other boundary cross right away.

questions.simplification_notes: Used 'Earth limit' instead of 'planetary boundary'. Explained linked Earth parts make small changes grow fast.
concepts_tested: Safe operating space and planetary boundaries framework: how boundaries define limits to human impact and guide governance decisions.
Non-linear thresholds and tipping points: why crossing a boundary may trigger abrupt, large-scale environmental change.
Normative baseline of the Holocene stability: why that stability is used as a reference and how deviations influence policy and societal assumptions.

questions.question: Why does having workers do one job help companies make items for less money?

options.A: Workers do one job to make more items for less money.
options.B: Dividing work makes firms produce fewer items.
options.C: Specialization causes more costs.
options.D: Only bulk buying lowers costs.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simpler words with shorter sentences to keep the main idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1
Concept 2
Concept 3

questions.question: How does a fixed cost only new firms pay help firms keep prices high?

options.A: An entrant fixed cost raises entrant average cost above incumbents' cost.
options.B: It raises every firm's marginal cost equally.
options.C: It makes buyers think quality is higher.
options.D: New firms can easily pay the cost.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and short sentences, kept entrant cost raising entrant disadvantage.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: An entrant-specific fixed cost (barrier to entry) creates an incumbent advantage and helps sustain monopoly or oligopoly power.
Concept 2: Barriers to entry distort prices and competition, which has implications for antitrust policy and market regulation.
Concept 3: Different sources of barriers (natural like brand loyalty vs. government-imposed or resource-based barriers) shape how markets function and how policy should respond.

questions.question: How do random choices change how sure we are about answers?

options.A: Random choices change the input data.
options.B: Random choices make results likely, not guaranteed.
options.C: Random choices guarantee termination.
options.D: Random choices make the result the same every run.

questions.simplification_notes: I used easy words. I used short sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of instructions that, starting from an initial state/input, proceeds through a finite number of states to produce output and terminate.
Concept 2: Algorithms can be deterministic or involve randomness (randomized algorithms), leading to probabilistic outcomes.
Concept 3: Distinction between algorithms and heuristics: algorithms have precise definitions and termination guarantees, whereas heuristics do not guarantee correctness or optimality.

questions.question: Why do regular checks of your own thinking help you solve problems?

options.A: They give you more facts automatically.
options.B: They help you check thinking for bias, gaps, and wrong conclusions.
options.C: They let many people decide for you.
options.D: They stop you from asking questions.

questions.simplification_notes: I simplified the question into child words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Critical thinking as analysis and evaluation of facts, evidence, observations, and arguments, including recognizing assumptions and justifying ideas while assessing rationality and consequences.
Concept 2: Critical thinking as a set of self-directed, self-disciplined, self-monitored, and self-corrective habits of the mind that require ownership and mindful communication/problem solving.
Concept 3: The role of an individual's knowledge base in determining the excellence of critical thinking and the notion that critical thinking can be learned or trained.

questions.question: In a common law system, why do earlier court decisions limit a judge? How can this limit be changed?

options.A: Precedents are written laws that judges must apply exactly.
options.B: Earlier decisions bind judges; higher courts, laws, or different facts can change them.
options.C: Precedent only persuade judges; no formal way exists to overturn them.
options.D: Precedent is a universal rule that binds all countries; changing them needs global agreement.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified language; preserved main idea with same correct answer.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mechanisms of law creation and sources (legislation, executive regulation, judicial decisions; role of precedent; constitutional influence)
Concept 2: Structure of legal systems (civil vs. common law; codification vs. case law; public vs. private law)
Concept 3: Law as a social mediator and normative project (how law influences politics, economics, society; aims of equality, fairness, justice)

questions.question: Why does a rule stay when people punish rule-breakers who get quick rewards?

options.A: People follow rules after they weigh costs or benefits.
options.B: People follow rules because rules match who they are.
options.C: Observers punish rule-breakers to scare others.
options.D: The behavior stays because most people keep choosing it.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to keep the idea clear.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Norm life cycle (emergence → cascade → internalization) and the processes that drive each stage (norm entrepreneurs, broad acceptance, taken-for-granted status).

Concept 2: Mechanisms for normative influence (logic of appropriateness vs. logic of consequences; third-party punishment; evolutionarily stable strategies) and how they explain compliance and stability.
Concept 3: Distinctions among norm types (regulative, constitutive, prescriptive) and how these categories relate to behavior, interests, and contextual variation.

questions.question: Why do people use tacit consent to show a government can rule? How does tacit consent differ from explicit consent?

options.A: People show tacit consent by staying under protection; explicit consent means saying yes.
options.B: Tacit consent equals explicit consent; both require signing a constitution.
options.C: Tacit consent means a government is legitimate just because people live there.
options.D: Tacit consent means being born in a land makes people obey forever.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I split the idea into two short questions.
concepts_tested: State of nature as the hypothetical starting condition that motivates forming a political order
Consent (explicit or tacit) as the mechanism by which individuals authorize authority
The exchange of freedoms for protection of rights and social order, underpinning the legitimacy of the state

questions.question: Why do we make future money worth less when adding costs and benefits today?

options.A: A discount rate shows money today is worth more than money later.
options.B: A discount rate only adjusts for price changes.
options.C: It makes all future money count the same.
options.D: It turns costs negative and benefits positive.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words. Kept the idea that money now is worth more.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Monetary valuation and time value of money (net present value) as the basis for comparing benefits and costs across time.
Concept 2: Using a cost–benefit ratio to rank options and determine which action increases welfare (and its relation to Pareto efficiency).
Concept 3: Limitations and uncertainties in CBA (accuracy of estimates, potential manipulation by interest groups) and their impact on decisions.

questions.question: Why do leaders need followers' help to reach a shared goal?

options.A: Formal power forces actions for short time only.
options.B: Social influence wins followers' lasting help.
options.C: Charisma alone always makes someone a good leader.
options.D: Followers help only if leaders use force.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words.
concepts_tested: Leadership as a process of social influence and a power-relationship between leaders and followers
Theoretical frameworks of leadership (traits, situational interaction, function, behavior, power, vision, values, charisma, intelligence) that explain how leadership operates
Ethico-cultural principles of leadership (intelligence, trustworthiness, humaneness, courage, discipline) as foundational virtues across traditions

questions.question: Why does 'fit for use' mean data quality depends on what someone needs?

options.A: People judge data by how well it meets needs for accuracy, speed, detail.
options.B: Because a single rule makes all data work the same everywhere.
options.C: Because cleaning data alone makes it good for any use.
options.D: Because rules can force one standard that makes data good for everything.

questions.simplification_notes: I replaced technical words with simple concrete examples.
concepts_tested: Fitness for use: data quality is determined by how well data meet the intended use in context (operations, decision making, planning).
Conformance to standards and quality dimensions: data quality involves inherent characteristics and adherence to defined requirements or specifications.
Governance and cleansing as mechanisms: data governance, standardization, and data cleansing are processes used to achieve and maintain data quality across multiple data sources.

questions.question: How does giving power to local people make projects last longer?

options.A: It gives decision power to outside groups.
options.B: It builds local ownership.
options.C: It cuts off outside help.
options.D: It makes only experts choose solutions.

questions.simplification_notes: Used shorter words and simple sentences.
concepts_tested: Empowerment and joint decision-making as core mechanisms and goals of participatory development (how empowerment leads to ownership and sustainable outcomes).
Competing perspectives on participation (Social Movement Perspective vs Institutional Perspective) and how they shape power relations, roles of outsiders, and aims of PD.
A staged project life cycle (Research, Design, Implementation, Evaluation) as a framework for incorporating stakeholder inputs and guiding participatory processes.

questions.question: Why must people use renewable resources at or below the rate they regrow? What rules should leaders make to keep use within that regrowth rate?

options.A: Leaders should limit use to keep extraction below regrowth.
options.B: Leaders should not make rules for renewable extraction.
options.C: Leaders should ignore limits because technology always finds substitutes.
options.D: Leaders should prioritize renewables only after non-renewables run out.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simpler words. I split the idea into two short questions.
concepts_tested: Renewable vs non-renewable resources and sustainability: how renewability determines use rates, replenishment, and policy implications.

Socio-political and environmental impacts of resource extraction and responses: how extraction drives conflicts and harms, and how sustainable development goals and circular economy aim to address these issues.
Classification frameworks for resources (origin and stage of development): how biotic vs abiotic origins and potential resources classifications guide assessment, discovery, and policy decisions.

questions.question: Why must the price of a nonrenewable resource rise at the interest rate?

options.A: An owner can sell now or wait to earn the market interest.
options.B: Future demand must grow at the interest rate.
options.C: Costs to get the resource rise at the interest rate.
options.D: Governments set price growth equal to the interest rate.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I kept the owner choice to sell now or wait.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Sustainability and ecological constraints as governing principles for economic activity (economy operating within Earth's natural resources; interdependence with natural ecosystems)
Concept 2: Depletion and pricing dynamics governed by Hotelling-like models (nonrenewable resource extraction paths; price rising with time at a rate linked to interest rate)
Concept 3: Non-market valuation and welfare considerations in resource economics (recreational vs commercial values; incorporation into welfare theory and environmental policy)

questions.question: How does moving non-urgent work to slow times help?

options.A: It moves non-urgent tasks to slow times to spread work, avoid overload.
options.B: It keeps every resource busy every period to get lowest cost, best service.
options.C: It locks resources to tasks forever, destroys service levels.
options.D: It sends all resources into inventory, stops service meeting demand.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the question simple and short. Used plain words and short option sentences. Kept the main idea that moving non-urgent tasks spreads work to avoid overload and cut urgent costs.
concepts_tested: Resource leveling as a mechanism to balance resource demand/supply over time while meeting service levels and minimizing cost.
Resources as stored capabilities with a development dimension: investing in existing resources to build new capabilities and unleashing them as demanded.
Corporate resource management and prioritization: focusing resources and abandoning less promising initiatives to prevent fragmentation and over-allocation across projects.

questions.question: Why do we need a model to link a small sample to a whole group? What happens if the model we use is wrong?

options.A: A model links the sample to the whole group; wrong models give wrong conclusions.
options.B: The data alone decide the group truth; model choice does not matter.
options.C: A model always fixes every bias; larger samples always correct model errors.
options.D: Models are not needed; sample summaries tell us the whole group facts.

questions.simplification_notes: Split the original complex question into two simple questions. Used plain words and short sentences. Kept the idea that models link samples to populations and that wrong models give wrong conclusions.
concepts_tested: The role of a statistical model in converting data into population-level conclusions (model-based inference)
The importance of sampling and underlying assumptions for drawing inferences about a population
The different forms of inferential propositions (point estimates, confidence intervals, credible intervals, hypothesis tests) and what they represent

questions.question: Why does pretend play help children learn words, rules, symbols?

options.A: It makes children repeat words alone to memorize them.
options.B: Adults help children use words, follow rules, use symbols.
options.C: It reduces thinking by letting children use only body movements.
options.D: It makes children play alone until they learn language without help.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified the wording for third graders. Kept the idea that adults guide children in play to teach language, rules.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Mechanisms of learning (habituation, classical conditioning, operant conditioning) and how each leads to changes in behavior or knowledge.
Concept 2: The role of play and social interaction (e.g., Vygotsky's view) in learning, including how play facilitates language, rule understanding, and symbol use.
Concept 3: Memory systems and their relation to learning (declarative vs procedural; episodic vs semantic) and how different memory types influence what is learned and how it is retrieved.

questions.question: Why do people respond more to things that look like a learned cue? Why do they respond less when things look less similar?

options.A: Learning links shared parts to stronger responses.
options.B: The learner keeps exact memories only.
options.C: The learner learns one rule for every situation.
options.D: Response strength depends only on reward frequency.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed technical words and details. Used simple words like shared parts and stronger responses.
concepts_tested: Transfer of learned patterns/abstractions to new, similar situations (principle of generalization)
Mechanisms: abstraction and connectionist processes that enable categorization and generalization
Generalization gradient: relationship between stimulus similarity and the strength of the response, used to measure generalization

questions.question: Why do powerful people get more water than poor farmers?

options.A: Different land or weather causes unequal water.
options.B: Power decides who gets more water.
options.C: Chance makes some communities suffer more.
options.D: People agree to the water plan.

questions.simplification_notes: Made the scenario short to ask about power.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Unequal distribution of environmental costs and benefits due to political, social, and economic differences.
Concept 2: Any change in environmental conditions tends to affect the political and economic status quo, reshaping power relations.
Concept 3: The politicization of environmental issues and the integration of ecological study with political economy, including how ownership rules and local institutions mediate broader pressures.

questions.question: How do small countries working together make a big country follow their shared goals?

options.A: They force the big country to accept a veto on all actions.
options.B: They form a binding group that raises the big country's cost to reach goals.
options.C: They use public opinion to shame the big country without real policy threats.
options.D: They isolate the big country from others to force it to give in.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words to show small countries teaming up to raise costs.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Multilateralism as a governance mechanism that coordinates national policies among three or more states to discourage unilateral action and bind powerful actors.
Concept 2: The Lilliputian strategy—how small powers coalition-build in multilateral settings to influence larger powers and achieve shared objectives (including burden-sharing).
Concept 3: Core theoretical principles (indivisibility and diffuse reciprocity) and the role of US or great-power context in shaping how multilateral arrangements function.

questions.question: Why do measurements that ignore part names stay the same after turning the shape?

options.A: The turn changes the shape, changing measurements.
options.B: The turn maps the shape onto itself, keeping measurements the same.
options.C: The turn changes distances between pairs of parts.
options.D: Symmetry needs a mirror rather than a turn.

questions.simplification_notes: Simplified language to ask why unlabeled measures stay constant under a turn.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Invariance under a transformation defines symmetry—an object is symmetric if a transformation (e.g., reflection, rotation, translation) leaves its overall shape unchanged.

Concept 2: Different symmetry types correspond to specific transformations (line/plane reflection, rotation, translation, glide reflection, rotoreflection, scale/helical symmetry), allowing classification by how parts relate under those transformations.

Concept 3: Symmetry as a cross-domain principle—present in mathematics, science/nature, and the arts, and even in abstract structures like logic—indicating deep connections through invariance and balance.

questions.question: Why does calling rings, fields, and vector spaces groups with extra rules help math?

options.A: The group rule alone decides everything.
options.B: Viewing them as groups lets mathematicians use group methods to study extra rules.
options.C: The group view does not matter for rings or fields.
options.D: This idea only works if the set is finite.

questions.simplification_notes: Kept the main idea that groups give the core and extra rules show interactions.
concepts_tested: The idea that many mathematical structures can be viewed as groups with additional operations and axioms, providing a unifying framework.
The role of symmetry groups and representation theory in connecting group theory to applications in physics, chemistry, and materials science.
The Erlangen program as an organizing principle of geometry, linking geometric concepts to group actions.

questions.question: Why does a drug changing into its mirror image in body make tests harder?

options.A: This change forces tests of both mirror forms plus how fast they change.
options.B: The change alters the drug weight in the body.
options.C: This change happens only in test tubes.
options.D: The change makes new shapes with other body targets.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced technical words with simple phrases about mirror image changes.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Relationship between stereoisomers (enantiomers vs diastereomers, and epimers) and how their different spatial arrangements lead to distinct physical/chemical and biological properties.
Concept 2: Dynamic stereochemistry and racemization, including how in vivo interconversion can affect drug safety and necessitate regulatory testing.
Concept 3: Tools and conventions for describing stereochemistry (CIP priority rules and Fischer projections) that enable unambiguous communication of stereochemical configurations.

questions.question: Why does removing duplicate parts of data let us shrink files? How does looking at the smaller file help us find useful knowledge?

options.A: Duplicate parts add unique signals that we must keep when shrinking files.
options.B: Shrinking files removes duplicate parts to keep the main patterns.
options.C: Optimal shrinking keeps every bit to prevent losing information.
options.D: Duplicate parts can make data seem more random.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Information as meaning derived through interpretation; information is not the same as knowledge, and information processing is iterative (data → information → knowledge).
Concept 2: The relationship between data, information, and knowledge, including how redundancy leads to compression toward an optimal limit and how analysis extracts usable knowledge.
Concept 3: Encoding, transmission, and transformation of information across forms and channels (including encryption for safe storage/communication).

questions.question: Why does $L=\{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\}$ show that simple state machines cannot check matching counts?

options.A: A stack machine uses the stack to match each a with each b.
options.B: A simple state machine can track all counts for any number n.
options.C: Only a very powerful machine can decide this language.
options.D: A guessing state machine can backtrack to match counts.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I replaced hard terms with easy phrases.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Transition mechanism — an automaton changes state based on the current state and input symbol via a transition function.
Concept 2: Automata as representations of formal languages — finite automata recognize formal languages, linking machine models to language theory.
Concept 3: Chomsky hierarchy as a framework — different classes of automata correspond to nested classes of languages, organizing their expressive power and capabilities.

questions.question: Why does $N^*N = NN^*$ let N act like multiplication on some $L^2$ space?

options.A: Normal operators always have a short list of special vectors.
options.B: Normality makes N act like multiplication on each part of the space.
options.C: We can change any operator bit by bit to look diagonal.
options.D: Normal operators only have point numbers in their spectrum.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I showed how normality lets N act by multiplication.
concepts_tested: Spectral theorem: conditions under which an operator can be diagonalized or represented by multiplication operators; connection to commutative C*-algebras and spectral decomposition.
Normal operators: definition via commutation with the adjoint (NN* = N*N) and their central role because the spectral theorem applies to them.

Representation and dimensionality: how diagonalization and spectral decomposition differ between finite-dimensional spaces and infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, including the idea of modeling operators via multiplication.

questions.question: Why does democracy use rules with judges to protect small groups?

options.A: A written rulebook plus neutral judges protect small groups from unfair laws.
options.B: The majority can ignore small groups' protections.
options.C: Only judges make the laws in this case.
options.D: Courts stop elections by taking permanent power.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard phrases with simple words such as rules, judges, small groups.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Liberal democracy balances majority rule with minority rights through a constitutional framework and an independent judiciary.
Concept 2: Direct democracy vs. representative democracy as distinct mechanisms for citizen participation and power delegation.
Concept 3: The relationship between democracy and legitimacy/outcomes (e.g., health, education, economic outcomes) and the role of public opinion in sustaining democratic systems.

questions.question: If school teaches thinking and checking ideas, what does the teacher do?

options.A: The teacher helps students check evidence, spot bias, and change beliefs.
options.B: The teacher makes students memorize one set of true ideas first.
options.C: The teacher avoids hard topics to prevent student confusion.
options.D: The teacher makes students memorize facts instead of teaching thinking.

questions.simplification_notes: I shortened the question. I used simple child words. I kept the original idea about teacher role change.
concepts_tested: Descriptive vs normative theories of education and how they influence educational practice
Epistemic aims of education: transmission of true beliefs versus development of reasoning/critical thinking
Education and power/equality: how schooling relates to state authority, discrimination, and wealth distribution

questions.question: Why study people, steps, and tools together instead of fixing just the tools?

options.A: System behavior comes from people, steps, and tools working together, not from one part.
options.B: Technology alone controls how well the system works.
options.C: Focusing only on rules solves system problems.
options.D: Only technical scores matter for system success.

questions.simplification_notes: Removed hard words. Used short, child friendly sentences.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Socio-technical systems perspective—addressing systemic problems by considering the interactions between people, organizations, and information systems.
Concept 2: Stakeholder-centric problem framing—understanding problems from the perspective of involved stakeholders and applying information technologies accordingly.
Concept 3: Transdisciplinary foundations—integration across technical, organizational, and human dimensions to bridge different domains.

questions.question: Why does mixing science and stories help people solve nature problems?

options.A: It ignores scientific facts.
options.B: It combines facts with values and cultures to make better plans for nature.
options.C: It uses one method only.
options.D: It slows progress with extra views.

questions.simplification_notes: Kept core idea of joining facts with values and cultures for better action.
concepts_tested: Interdisciplinary synthesis: bridging sciences and humanities to generate new ways of thinking about environmental problems.
Rejection of the nature–culture divide: environmental issues are entangled with justice, labor, politics, and human meaning.
Plural epistemologies and inclusivity: incorporating Western, Eastern, and Indigenous perspectives to reshape understanding of the environment.

questions.question: What duties come from saying nature has value on its own?

options.A: It gives nature moral status regardless of human benefits.
options.B: It makes duties about making people as happy as possible.
options.C: It ties duties only to how useful nature is to people.
options.D: It uses money value of nature to decide which actions to take.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words with short sentences to keep the main idea.
concepts_tested: Intrinsic vs. instrumental value of the environment and how this distinction grounds environmental ethics
Rights of nature and moral status of non-human entities (e.g., rivers, ecosystems) and associated ethical considerations
The relationship between humans, technology, and the natural world, and how ethical theories translate into responses to environmental challenges (climate change, conservation, restoration, policy)

questions.question: Why does using talks with people and industry numbers help more than one source?

options.A: It lowers how much information you need.
options.B: Combining talks and numbers gives clearer checked results.
options.C: It makes sure the study shows the whole market.
options.D: It makes results only facts with no opinion.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced technical words with simple phrases like talks and industry numbers.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Market research is a systematic, organized process to gather and interpret information about target markets and customers to support decision making and maintain competitiveness.

Concept 2: It employs a mix of qualitative and quantitative methods (primary research) and also uses secondary data, with distinctions between qualitative vs. quantitative and primary vs. secondary research.

Concept 3: Market research and marketing research are related but distinct fields (markets and distribution vs. marketing processes), and confusion between them highlights conceptual boundaries relevant to business strategy.

questions.question: How do hidden rules at school change students' learning?

options.A: Teachers add extra facts not in the planned lessons.
options.B: Hidden rules shape students' effort, focus, and view of feedback.
options.C: Hidden rules replace planned lessons.
options.D: Hidden rules make test scores the same across classrooms.

questions.simplification_notes: I used 'hidden rules' to mean hidden curriculum.
concepts_tested: Explicit vs implicit (hidden) curriculum: how non-taught aspects of the school environment and culture influence learning outcomes alongside the formal curriculum.
Curriculum as the total student experience: how planned content, instructional materials, processes, and assessment collectively align with and realize educational objectives.
Standardization vs autonomy in curriculum design: how centralized/national curricula vs. teacher/learner autonomy affect implementation, innovation, and student experiences.

questions.question: What do written rules do in a country with both central and local governments?

options.A: The central government makes almost all rules for the whole country.
options.B: The constitution gives local governments local powers while the union handles national matters.
options.C: Regional governments may override central laws whenever they choose.
options.D: The country ends local governments when leaders reach a nationwide agreement.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened text to simple words while showing split powers preserving unity.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Division of powers between central/federal and regional governments as the core mechanism and principle of federalism.
Concept 2: Federalism as a middle-ground relationship on a spectrum between regional separation and regional integration, distinguishing it from confederalism and unitary states.
Concept 3: The multi-level governance relationship: how constitutional allocation of competences enables autonomy at sub-national levels while maintaining a unified system.

questions.question: Why do archives keep records of where items come from and who handled them?

options.A: Archivists record provenance and custody details for proof of origin and for user access.
options.B: Storing items in cool rooms alone proves they are real.
options.C: Removing context makes records easier to use.
options.D: File format alone proves a digital file is authentic.

questions.simplification_notes: I used short simple words and one short question for kids.
concepts_tested: Authenticity and trustworthiness of archival records: why records must be what they claim to be and how archivists ensure reliability, integrity, and usability.
The archival workflow as a mechanism: how appraisal, storage/preservation, and processing (arranging and describing) interact to preserve materials and enable access.

The impact of digital technology on archival science: how the advent of digital documents and databases prompts re-evaluation of methods and the relationship between traditional practices and digital curation.

questions.question: Why do we keep some papers as records that show who did what?

options.A: People keep them on purpose as proof of actions with context.
options.B: Any transaction paper becomes a record kept forever.
options.C: Records must always be digital to keep their proof value.
options.D: Law alone defines records regardless of context or purpose.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words. Kept idea that records prove actions and context.
concepts_tested: Records as evidence that supports accountability and governance
The transactional/contextual nature of records: not all documents are records; records are persistent representations of activities requiring conscious retention
Life-cycle management and control mechanisms: creation/receipt, classification, storage, retrieval, tracking, and disposition to manage risk and compliance

questions.question: Why does sorting items in many ways help more users than a fixed order?

options.A: It forces users to follow one fixed order.
options.B: Sorting things by several groups with computers gives flexible, fast search.
options.C: It uses only keyword search.
options.D: It makes navigation the same for every user.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard words with simple words. Kept the idea that many-way sorting with computers helps users.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The relationship and overlap between library science and information science and how this relationship influences professional practice.
Concept 2: The mechanism by which information resources are organized and classified to serve diverse user groups, including the interaction with classification systems and technology.
Concept 3: The ethical, legal, and political economy considerations that govern library and information services and their impact on practice.

questions.question: Why do people choose some old things to call heritage? How do those choices change which things get saved?

options.A: Communities save every old thing equally based only on technical ease.
options.B: Power, values, and group identity shape heritage choices by guiding protection and funding.
options.C: International law sets a single fixed list for heritage.
options.D: People save things only for tourism money.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and short sentences to keep the main idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Heritage as a selective social construct — not all past legacies are heritage; society selects what to preserve or recognize as heritage.
Concept 2: Typology of heritage and its implications — tangible, intangible, and natural heritage require different preservation approaches and disciplines.

Concept 3: Preservation/conservation as an active, governance-related process — involving laws, international agreements, institutions, and the link to economic value and tourism, illustrating cause-effect relationships between protection and societal outcomes.

questions.question: Why does a math or computer model help find the key parts that control life? How does the model let us predict what will happen in new cases?

options.A: It makes the data more complex.
options.B: It replaces experiments by copying reality.
options.C: It shows parts with their rules that control the system to predict new results.
options.D: It copies observed data exactly.

questions.simplification_notes: Made words short to show model purpose.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Modeling as a tool to reveal governing principles of biological systems and to enable prediction through quantitative representations.
Concept 2: The relationship and distinction between theoretical biology and mathematical biology (principles-focused vs. tools-focused) and their overlap.
Concept 3: The role of mathematics in biology driven by system complexity, including the development of new techniques and cross-disciplinary insights.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why people choose ADR instead of going to court?

options.A: They mainly affect court enforcement of decisions.
options.B: Lower costs, speed, privacy, control make ADR better when those matter.
options.C: They guarantee faster results in all cases.
options.D: Privacy is the only reason people choose ADR.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words instead of legal terms. I kept the drivers cost, speed, privacy, control.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Why ADR is adopted and sustained (drivers such as reduced costs, faster resolution, confidentiality, and parties’ control over decision-makers) and how these factors influence dispute-resolution choices.

Concept 2: How ADR interacts with the court system (compulsory/ordered ADR, NCDR vs ADR, and judicial decisions like Halsey v Milton Keynes Trust and Churchill v Merthyr) and how legal frameworks shape ADR use.
Concept 3: How the design of ADR has evolved (from traditional guild-arbitrated disputes to market-based arbitrators) and the trade-offs between accessibility and community involvement.

questions.question: Why is it wrong to say religion weakens the same way everywhere?

options.A: Different measures of belief, belonging, and practice show religion changes under modern life.
options.B: Belief, belonging, and practice always decline together everywhere.
options.C: Religion only matters to government.
options.D: Secular change always follows a straight line.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words and short sentences to show varied measures of religion.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Secularization thesis as a mechanism linking modernization/rationalization/science to diminished religious authority in social life and governance.
Concept 2: Desecularization as a counter-trend involving resurgence of religion in certain contexts (e.g., Islamic revival, post-Soviet Russia).

Concept 3: Non-universality and complexity of secularization, including debates about measurement (belief, belonging, practice), boundaries between religion and secular, and possible post-secular models.

questions.question: Why follow a rule that usually helps, even if it sometimes makes things worse?

options.A: Because guessing outcomes for each action is hard.
options.B: Because the rule itself can be right even if results are bad.
options.C: Because people like rules for their simplicity.
options.D: Because it brings quick rewards in every situation.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording using simple cause-effect words for children.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The principle that the rightness of an act is determined by its consequences (outcome-based evaluation and balance of good over evil).
Concept 2: The goods that count for evaluating consequences (pleasure, absence of pain, preference satisfaction, or a broader general good) and how different definitions shape judgments.

Concept 3: The relationships and potential compatibilities between consequentialism and other ethical frameworks (deontology, virtue ethics, pragmatic ethics), including examples of how they might overlap or be reconciled (e.g., rule-consequentialism, rights justified by consequences).

questions.question: Why do duty rules judge actions by duty not by results? How would mixing duty rules with good character fix bad duty-only outcomes?

options.A: Duties and rights give firm rules not based on results with character guiding duties.
options.B: Results decide what is right with a mix using character more than duties.
options.C: Character alone guides action with a mix dropping duties.
options.D: Social contracts decide what is right with a mix replacing duty by social good.

questions.simplification_notes: Replaced hard terms with simple words about duties, results, and character.
concepts_tested: The three main normative theories (virtue ethics, deontological ethics, consequentialism): what each emphasizes (character, duties/rights, outcomes) and the idea that hybrids can combine elements.
Normative ethics as prescriptive and its relationship to other branches (how it differs from metaethics, applied ethics, descriptive ethics) and why that matters for evaluating actions.

Foundational frameworks within the theories (e.g., Kant’s categorical imperative, Rawls’ contract theory with a veil of ignorance, natural rights theories) and how these serve as mechanisms or principles to determine right action.

questions.question: Why does help from local, national, and global groups make nature rules work better?

options.A: It gives one group all power to act faster.
options.B: Sharing knowledge, money, tests, and accountability across levels helps rules work.
options.C: One government can force the same rules in every place.
options.D: It removes the need for talking between levels.

questions.simplification_notes: This version uses simple words about local, national, and global help.
concepts_tested: Multi-level, multi-actor governance: interactions among state, market, and civil society across local, national, and global levels in formulating and implementing environmental policies.
Embedding the environment in decision-making: ensuring environmental considerations permeate all levels of policy and action, and recognizing the connection between people and ecosystems.
Transition to circular/sustainable systems: moving from cradle-to-grave waste models to cradle-to-cradle/closed-loop approaches within governance to promote sustainable development.

questions.question: Why can fixing one part fail to help the whole system in ecosystem thinking?

options.A: Fixing one part can harm the system by changing material and energy flows.
options.B: The ecosystem idea says each part works alone.
options.C: Saying nature has no waste makes industrial waste seem unimportant.
options.D: Policies and markets always turn small fixes into big benefits.

questions.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, used simple words, kept idea of flows, feedbacks, and system effects.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Closed-loop / industrial metabolism — shifting from open-loop waste generation to loops where wastes become inputs for new processes, highlighting material and energy flow as a systemic phenomenon.
Concept 2: Ecosystem-inspired, systems-based view — understanding emergent behavior and interdependencies of industrial and natural systems, using ecosystem metaphors to guide sustainable design.

Concept 3: Life-cycle thinking and eco-design (including product stewardship) — designing and evaluating products and processes across their entire life cycle to reduce environmental impact and decarbonize/dematerialize.

questions.question: Why do historians' source and method choices change how we see the past?

options.A: It makes history fully true and stops all argument.
options.B: It has no effect on how people tell facts.
options.C: Historians' methods act like lenses that filter evidence and change stories.
options.D: It only changes the writing style and not the facts.

questions.simplification_notes: Used simple words and kept the idea that methods and sources change history.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The methodological foundations of historiography (how sources, research techniques, and theoretical frameworks shape historical interpretation)
Concept 2: The historical evolution of historiography (from ancient to modern periods, including professionalization and incorporation of social science perspectives)
Concept 3: The influence of historians’ identities and loyalties on historiography (debate about bias and perspective in historical writing)

questions.question: How does the central executive help the mind solve new problems?

options.A: It stores information passively.
options.B: It pulls memories from long-term memory into working memory to reach goals.
options.C: It stores all new information straight into long-term memory.
options.D: It controls development only in fixed stages.

questions.simplification_notes: I used simple words. I kept the main idea about the central executive moving memories to reach goals.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: The information processing sequence and the roles of attention, working memory, encoding, and long-term memory in transforming input into output.
Concept 2: The central executive as the control system that coordinates retrieval and manipulation of information across working and long-term memory.
Concept 3: The continuous development view, where cognitive maturation changes processing components over time, contrasting with stage-based theories like Piaget’s.

questions.question: How do DNA tags near a gene start lower gene reading? Why can cells keep the same tags after copying?

options.A: It mutates DNA letters that pass to daughter cells.
options.B: It brings proteins that open DNA for more gene reading.
options.C: Chemical tags at a gene start attract proteins that close DNA in daughter cells.
options.D: It changes DNA letters to stop the gene permanently.

questions.simplification_notes: I replaced technical terms with simple words and kept the main idea.
concepts_tested: Concept 1: Regulation can act at multiple stages of gene expression, with transcription initiation frequently being the primary control point, linking signals to transcriptional output.
Concept 2: Epigenetic regulation (chromatin structure, histone modifications, DNA methylation, ncRNA) modulates gene accessibility and expression, with potential heritability.
Concept 3: Gene regulatory networks and hierarchical regulation—one regulator controls others, creating cascades that drive cellular differentiation and complex developmental programs.

questions.question: Why can a drug that binds tightly not always work well in the body? How do body handling and target response together decide the final effect?
